ELECTRICIAN ONBOARD MEETING CONDUCTED WITH MASTER/CE PRIOR TO TROUBLE SHOOTING CRANE NO. 3  1720LT – SHIPS ENGINEERS WITH THE LOCAL ELECTRICIAN PROCEED WITH THE TROUBLE SHOOTING OF CRANE NO. 3  1810LT – LOCAL SHORE ELECTRICIAN REPORTED THAT CRANE NO. 3 DOES NOT HAVE AN ELECTRICAL PROBLEM AND INFORMED ANOTHER SHORE TECHNICIAN(MECHANICAL/HYDRAULIC) WILL BE ONBOARD FOR FURTHER TROUBLESHOOTINGS ON CRANE NO. 3  1854LT – LOCAL SHORE TECHNICIN(MECHANICAL/HYDRAULIC) ONBOARD  1900LT-1915LT – A SHORT TOOLBOX MEETING, WORK PLAN DISCUSSION AND RA CONDUCTED PRIOR TO COMMENCED ANOTHER ASPECTS OF CRANE NO. 3 TROUBLE SHOOTING WITH THE LOCAL SHORE TECHNICIAN(MECHANICAL/HYDRAULIC).  1920LT – COMMENCED TORUBLESHOOTING OF CRANE NO. 3  2115LT – CRANE NO. 3 JIB FELL DOWN HITTING THE STBD SIDE FIXED LOG STANCHION POST AND THE FOREWARD PANEL OF HATCH COVER (OPENED POSITION) CAUSING A SIGNIFICANT DAMAGED TO CRANE NO 3 JIB AND PORTION OF FOREWARD PANEL OF HATCH COVER NO 4 (OPENED POSITION) AS SHOWN ON THE ATTACHED PHOTOS AND CONFIRMED NOBODY FROM STEVEDORES/SHIPS CREW/SHORE TECHINICIAN WAS HURT DURING THE INCIDENT  2115LT- STEVEDORES STOPPED LOADING OPERATION DUE TO CRANE NO. 3 INCIDENT
ImmediateAction: 1)SITE ASSESSMENTS MUSTER ALL PERSONNEL WORKING ON THE AREA INCLUDING STEVEDORES AND OTHER SHORE PERSONNEL  2)PHOTOS AND EVIDENCE TAKEN FOR THE REPORTS  3)INTERVIEW WITH THE CREW ON SITE FOR THE REPAIR OF CRANE NO. 3 BEFORE THE INCIDENT  4)COORDINATE WITH TECHNICAL SUPERINTENDEDNT FOR POSSIBLE CLASS/MAKER ATTENDANCE  5) COORDINATE/COMMUNICATE WITH LOCAL AGENT FOR NECESSARY LOCAL REQUIREMENTS SPECIALLY FROM PORT AUTHORITIES.    ALL DOCUMENTS AND EVIDENCE SENT SEPARATELY DUE TO TO LARGE FILES AND EXCEEDING CAPACITY OF ATTACHMENTS IN THIS REPORT FORM
DirectCause: The removal of the winch motor directly resulted in the brake disengaging from the shaft, secondary supports failing to hold the weight and the jib descending till it came to rest on the timber stanchions abreast of the crane and part of the hatch cover.  Lack of precise directions in manual for motor removal and description of the mechanical brake holding in case of hydraulic failure possibly leading to assumption that the hydraulic system was supplemented by an additional mechanical system.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Before dismantle/removed any machinery/parts of the Crane, the jib must be in stow position/Jib rest, before starting any job carried out Tool box meeting with all parties involve with risk assessment and etc and always follow/consult repair maintenance instruction by Makers.
Corrective Action: Before dismantle/removed any machinery/parts of the Crane, the jib must be in stow position/Jib rest, before starting any job carried out Tool box meeting with all parties involve with risk assessment and etc and always follow/consult repair maintenance instruction by Makers.
ClosingNote: Investigation report has been submitted and corrective action followed up. To be discussed during safety meeting

IncidentDetails: At 1010LT 17 Apr 2019, while conducting lifeboat drill at Kwinana (G2) anchorage area and water bourne testing of portside lifeboat stbd side window of portside lifeboat damaged.Due to sudden swell the portside life boat rides on its crest and ships list to port causing the boatfall and the remote counter weight was caught in between ships side and the lifeboat window.
IncidentEventDetails: Due to sudden swell the portside life boat rides on its crest and ships list to port causing the boatfall and the remote counter weight was caught in between ships side and the lifeboat window.
ImmediateAction: No injury caused,ceased the drill and secure the lifeboat/equiptments/remove the remote counter weight while recovering the portside lifeboat.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Due to sudden swell the portside life boat rides on its crest and ships list to port causing the boatfall and the remote counter weight was caught in between ships side and the lifeboat window.
DirectCause: Due to sudden swell the portside life boat rides on its crest and ships list to port causing the boatfall and the remote counter weight was caught in between ships side and the lifeboat window.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Remove and secure the remote wire/counter weight during lifeboat launching except on perfect sea condition and purpose of testing the remote launching operation.
Corrective Action: Remove and secure the remote wire/counter weight during lifeboat launching except on perfect sea condition and purpose of testing the remote launching operation.
ClosingNote: To be discussed during safety meeting and checked during routine inspection and prior to lowering the boat.

IncidentDetails: At 0930 LT  v/l was discharging at Longkou using NO3 COP through No2 manifold. Manifold pressure :  2 Bars  No1 & no2 discharge line was not in use and required valves were kept shut on deck on these lines . Cargo line had some cargo remaining in  the locked section of pipeline between the shut valves  and vapor expansion in the line resulted a mild seepage in one of the pump room flange of no1 line & deck flanges (2nos) of no.2 line.   Approx. 2.5ltr of mild spraying of cargo was observed on the pipeline around the flange and the  nearby deck area.  It was immediately cleaned with in 20mins by deck crew & was not noticed by any third party. This did not cause any operational delays nor did it  hamper our  vessel reputation by any means .  Clean up operation has been completed & flanges tightened with no signs of any further leaks found . Discharging operations was completed at 1300 LT as per schedule.
IncidentEventDetails: Date   : 11May 2019  Port    : Longkou  Cargo : ESPO crude   Manifold connection : No1 & No2 ( 2x16”)  Operation : Discharging  Berth # 24, Port side Along side  Cargo temperature in tanks: 13 Deg C .   Air temp: 18 Deg C     0930 LT :  v/l was discharging at Longkou using NO3 COP through No2 manifold. Manifold pressure :  2 Bars.  0932 LT : During deck rounds found No1 & no2 discharge line which was not in use and required valves were kept shut on deck on these lines due to vapour expansion in the line resulted a mild seepage in one of the pump room flange of no1 line & deck flanges (2nos) of no.2 line.  Approx. 2.5ltr of mild spraying of cargo was observed on the pipeline around the flange and the  nearby deck area.  0934 LT : Immediately lines de pressurized.  1000 LT : Clean up operation has been completed & flanges tightened with no signs of any further leaks found.
ImmediateAction: Immediately cargo lines de pressurized & clean up operation carried out.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Mild seepage in one of the pump room flange of no1 line & deck flanges (2nos) of no.2 line.
DirectCause: Procedure not followed.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Cargo lines not in use for longer period to be drained , depressurized & secured.
ClosingNote: If the line stripping is expected to take longer time, it is important that the valves be opened at frequent intervals to avoid pressurization due to vapor expansion.

IncidentDetails: A small Mixie used for making dough short circuited, and caught fire, due to the smoke fire alarm got activated
IncidentEventDetails: Chief Cook was making Dosa batter in the small kitchen Mixie, when he smelt plastic burning and saw smoke coming out of the mixie, he immidiately unplugged the power cable and covered the Mixie with fire Blanket. Pre alarm got activated on the bridge and chief officer rushed down to the galley to investigate.   By then the chief cook had covered it with Fire blanket and the fire had subsided and the mixie plastic cover had got warped due to the heat of the burning motor inside.
ImmediateAction: Disconnecetd the power cable and covered it with Fire blanket.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Approx 200$
DirectCause: Inadequate maintenance, Inadequate Supervision.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Crew should be briefed not to use equipment unattended, any abnormal sound or signs on a ageing machinery to be investigated and rectified, routine electrical and mechanical maintenance of all equipment should be conducted diligently
Corrective Action: Crew should be briefed not to use equipment unattended, any abnormal sound or signs on a ageing machinery to be investigated and rectified, routine electrical and mechanical maintenance of all equipment should be conducted diligently
ClosingNote: Job closed 16/05

IncidentDetails: Pumping -out of ballast was not able to keep up with the loading rate. Although the contract quantity was loaded, the maximum quantity that the vessel could load was not reached. Due to more ballast water unable to pump out that supposedly for additional cargo.
IncidentEventDetails: On the 7th of May 2019, vessel arrived in the port of Newcastle, Australia and berthed alongside Kooragang berth no. 6. Shortly after at 0848H/08 May 2019, cargo surveyor w/ foreman came onboard & met C/O at the ship’s office to conduct the initial draft survey. The exchange of information was also made between C/O & foreman. According to foreman the load rate was about 6,500mt/hr, of which C/O agreed since the figures coincides with the figures indicated on the load plan form they have sent to fill-up. Furthermore, expecting the de-ballast capacity rate to be 5,000mt/hr, with the small difference, C/O believed that we could catch-up w/ the load rate, because C/O would be pumping only on CH #6 for 2 loading sequences & besides the fact that CH #6 requires no stripping.    At 0911H/07 May 2019, loading operation started simultaneously w/ the pumping out of ballast. In spite the inspections from AMSA, Quarantine, NK surveyor & Divers, C/O's focus & monitoring was not hindered in any way. However, as loading progresses, C/O observed that loading rate was way higher than expected. Unfortunately, at around 1750H/07 May 2019, C/O informed the Master and requested permission, that due to the fast loading rate, the loading sequence be revised to prevent the vessel from having a trim by the head. Master confirms and makes the request from foreman for a temporary stoppage. Foreman agrees in return for just 1 hour and confirms the stoppage would be off the record. After an hour has passed, loading resumed and vessel has regained a favorable trim.    Sadly, at around 0100H/08 May 2019, due to the same reason of preventing the vessel from having a trim by the head, loading sequence was again revised as agreed w/ foreman and with Master’s confirmation. Vessel’s trim at the time was about 3.0m. In which C/O together with foreman continue to monitor the loading operation. At 0435H/08 May 2019, I re-checked the remaining ballast onboard. This was before the completion of CH #1. I calculated to confirm the remaining cargo to load, with still more ballast onboard and drafts were already reached. By this time, de-ballasting and stripping still continues with Master’s assistance, using only 1 pump, as air bound sets in if two pumps were used. De-ballasting still continues, but at a slow rate.    The remaining tanks with ballast water at the time with  less than 2 meters soundings were DBT #3 P/S, DBT #4 P/S & DBT #5 P/S, with a trim of more than 1 meter. In the previous loading operations, since C/O joined the vessel last Oct. 2018, I wasn’t able to use 2 pumps all the way, because previous loading operations took more than 3 days to complete. So, C/O was only using 1 pump to compensate with the loading rate at that time. C/O never observed that by a certain level of a little less than 2 meters, 2 pumps could no longer be productive in the mentioned tanks. And C/O was unaware that without an intermediate valve or cross-over valve between two ballast pump lines, could make a big help in pumping out ballast. C/O thought it could not affect the de-ballasting performance, since a situation never arise, until only recently.   At 0445H/08 May 2019, cargo surveyor was onboard and confirmed that more ballast was still to be pump-out. C/O called C/E to re-check valves and pump’s function on their side, and after the check, the reply was that all was working normally. Still with a slow rate using 1 pump, de-ballasting and stripping in other tanks continued. At 0625H/08 May 2019, surveyor together with foreman, stopped the de-ballasting operations, as per instructions from the terminal. We prepared for intermediate survey and C/O was informed by surveyor that no de-ballasting will be allowed until cargo completion. From 0630H~0650H/08 May 2019, intermediate survey resulted into remaining cargo to be loaded in CH #2 will be 4,400mt. After the intermediate draft checks, Master once again requested for another temporary stoppage of loading & continue the de-ballasting & stripping, since vessel’s departure was re-scheduled at 2100H/08 May 2019 high water. But Master’s request was denied, due to the reason that loader was scheduled to load another incoming vessel at berth no. 5.    At 0730H/8 May 2019, loading resumed and was completed at 0830H/08 May 2019. Final draft survey resulted to be 4,332mt of cargo received. At 0955H/08 May 2019 cargo surveyor disembarked the vessel together w/ foreman. As vessel was re-scheduled to depart at 2100H/08 May 2019 high water, it was only then that C/O continued to de-ballast & strip the remaining ballast using 1 pump and eductor to reduce the remaining ballast water on board.
ImmediateAction: Master assisted Chief Mate for de-ballasting operation advised to use one ballast pump only due to air bound experienced if using two ballast pumps and stripping by Eductor continued. Keep monitor the suction pressure and de-ballasting by one pump it’s working but unsteady suction was observed.  ON/OFF of using of one ballast pump vice versa despite very slow suction but stripping on other tank continued.
DirectCause: Improper handling of ballast operation and poor cargo planning.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: All stages of loading preparation should be well plan and check for compliance with port requirements.
Corrective Action: All stages of loading preparation should be well plan and check for compliance with port requirements.
Cause Analysis: Incorrect use of equipment or machinery
ClosingNote: Attached report submitted to Rightship. To be discussed in safety meeting.

IncidentDetails: 1P WBT Valve, 1PBM opening and shuting only 20%. ON INVESTIGATION FOUND VALVE CRACKED AND INOPERABLE.
ImmediateAction: 2 PORT AND 1PORT BALLAST TANKS DEBALLASTED. VALVE NO. 1 PBM OF 1 PORT WBT, WHICH IS LOCATED IN 2P WBT, INSPECTED AND FOUND CRACKED. VALVE REMOVED, CHECKED FOR REPAIRS AND REQUISITION FOR NEW VALVE RAISED.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1PORT WBT VALVE 1PBM FOUND CRACKED AND INOPERABLE. VALVE SHAFT FOUND BENT AND VALVE NOT REUSABLE AND REPAIR NOT POSSIBLE.
DirectCause: Incorrect use of Equipment,Lack of Knowledge, Inadequate supervision
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Officers and crew should be advised that proper precautions should be taken to avoid any water hammering, officers should be trained to ensure that empty ballast pipe during initial stages of ballasting should be filled up slowly ensuring no valve is shut which could get damaged due to water hammering.
Corrective Action: Officers and crew should be advised that proper precautions should be taken to avoid any water hammering, officers should be trained to ensure that empty ballast pipe during initial stages of ballasting should be filled up slowly ensuring no valve is shut which could get damaged due to water hammering.
ClosingNote: Job Closed 24/06

IncidentDetails: On 24th May 2019, at about 2200hrs, Oil leak was noticed from #1 D/G, same was stopped after starting the st-by D/G. No damage to environment, property and  injury to person.
IncidentEventDetails: On 24th May 2019, at about 2200hrs, duty motorman found abnormal smell while he was moping near the air bottle area, which is aft of the A/E platform and found Heavy fuel oil leaking from #1 A/E.
ImmediateAction: 1.Duty motort man informed duty engineer and D/E started #2 A/E and shifted load to # 2 A/E.  2.D/E stopped # 1 A/E and activated the quick closing valve from ECR for #1 A/E to stop the leak.  3.D/E informed 2/E and Chief Engineer.  4.Upon investigation found that the air vent plug of #1 A/E fuel oil port side filter was open.  5.Immediately the vent plug was closed.  6.The quick closing valve opened and engine tried out. Found engine running satisfactorily without any leaks.
DirectCause: #1 D/G F.O filter lagging was removed for cleaning the lagging and the body of the filter. During this time, vessel suspects, the vent valve might have been disturbed from the tight shut position. And while #1 D/G was on-load, vent valve opened up due to vibration.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: The vent cocks to be secured to prevent accidental opening
Corrective Action: The vent cocks to be secured to prevent accidental opening
ClosingNote: The vent cocks secured in closed position to prevent accidental opening by drilling a hole in the operating wheel and secured with a locking pin in closed position.

IncidentDetails: On a voyage from Nansha to Hongkong, vessel Maersk Edrine had instructions to stem maximum bunkers at Hong Kong.   In order to stem maximum bunker, vessel topped up MGO storage and service tanks to maximum levels 161.8 MT and 82.6 MT respectively.    On 16th May 2019, vessel carried out fuel change over for entering into ECA area. Vessel arrived Hongkong berth on 18th of May and bunkered MGO 410.480 MT in MGO Storage tank, MGO Serv tank and MDO Serv tank. Vessel bunkered up to 89% of the MGO storage tank. The high level alarms were set to activate at 86%. Up on completion of bunkering, the high level alarm for MGO Storage tank were in active condition.    The high-level alarm set point was not changed beyond 86% in alarm monitoring system after bunkering.    The MGO service tanks capacity is 106.8 M3 & MGO storage tank capacity is 209.8 M3. The venting of these tanks lead to a common air vent on to the deck at starboard side. The MGO service tank has overflow pipe leading to MGO storage tank, while the high level alarm is available in storage tank.     Vessel departed Hong Kong on 19th May with MGO storage tank in high level alarm condition. Vessel arrived and berthed at Yantian on 19th may at 1442 hrs LT.    On 20th May, 2019 @0753 Hours, Foreman reported oil spill on poop deck to chief officer and duty officer. Duty officer informed Master, immediately alarm raised and summoned emergency stations and informed ECR stating that there is oil spill on poop deck.  Found MGO Service and MGO storage tank level were completely full. There were no transfer pump running at that moment. All crew had started oil spill clean-up operation.    In order to reduce the level and stop overflowing from vent, transferred the MGO Storage tank to MDO Service tank. The leak on the vent stopped but the level of MGO serv tank continued to raise even after transfer and hence the GE MGO supply pump was stopped. The level on the MGO Storage and service tank remained stable. It was noted that the oil had flowed from the MDO service tank (in use) to the MGO service and storage tank.    The return from the Generator Engine had been set to the same tank (MDO serv. tank) through Valve No.205V. The same return line also serving to the MGO service tank through Valve 182V.During initial line up, it had been confirmed by the duty engineer that the valve 182V was in closed condition and only 205V was open.    Vessel dismantled the valve no 182V (SDNR) for further troubleshooting. It was found that the valve seat was not holding as intended, which was witnessed by the attending DNV-GL Surveyor. Valve was replaced as instructed by DNV-GL surveyor and found the MGO Service tank level to be stable for the 6 hours.    Further to above MGO & MDO tanks Air vents name plates were interchanged since yard delivery.     From the calculation it has been found that approximately 2.6 M3 of MGO overflown from Air vent. Out of Which 2.4 M3 collected in 12 drums of capacity 200 litre each (12 X 0.2 M3).  And 0.2 M3 (200 Litres) remains unaccounted on board. We suspect approximately 200 Litres may have escaped overboard to cause the oil sheen on the ship side & rest has contained as solid waste,  absorbed by the saw dust and the oil absorbent pads.
IncidentEventDetails: MGO STORAGE TANK WAS IN HIGH LEVEL ALARM CONDITION. ST-BY DISEL GENERATOR FUEL OIL SYSTEM LINED WITH MGO DIRECT SUPPLY SYSTEM. THE RETURN VALVE NO. 182V TO MGO SERVICE TANK FOUND LEAKING, THIS LEAD TO FILLING UP OF MGO SERVICE TANK WHICH IN TURN CAUSED MGO STORAGE TANK TO FILL UP TO MAXIMUM LEVEL LEADING TO OVERFLOW THROUGH AIR VENT ON DECK.
ImmediateAction: ALARM RAISED. INITIATED EMERGENCY ACTIONS AS PER SOPEP. REPORTED OIL SPILL TO SPRO, SHORE AUTHORITIES, AGENTS AND OFFICE. ONBOARD OIL SPILL CONTAINMENT/CLEAN-UP BY SHIP STAFF AND CLEAN-UP IN WATER BY SPRO BOAT.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: On a voyage from Nansha to Hongkong, vessel Maersk Edrine had instructions to stem maximum bunkers at Hong Kong.   In order to stem maximum bunker, vessel topped up MGO storage and service tanks to maximum levels 161.8 MT and 82.6 MT respectively.    On 16th May 2019, vessel carried out fuel change over for entering into ECA area. The high-level alarm set point was not changed beyond 86% in alarm monitoring system after bunkering.    The MGO service tanks capacity is 106.8 M3 & MGO storage tank capacity is 209.8 M3. The venting of these tanks lead to a common air vent on to the deck at starboard side. Vessel departed Hong Kong on 19th May with MGO storage tank in high level alarm condition. Vessel arrived and berthed at Yantian on 19th may at 1442 hrs LT. The leak on the vent stopped but the level of MGO serv tank continued to raise even after transfer and hence the GE MGO supply pump was stopped. The level on the MGO Storage and service tank remained stable. It was noted that the oil had flowed from the MDO service tank (in use) to the MGO service and storage tank.    The return from the Generator Engine had been set to the same tank (MDO serv. tank) through Valve No.205V. The same return line also serving to the MGO service tank through Valve 182V.During initial line up, it had been confirmed by the duty engineer that the valve 182V was in closed condition and only 205V was open.    Vessel dismantled the valve no 182V (SDNR) for further troubleshooting. It was found that the valve seat was not holding as intended, which was witnessed by the attending DNV-GL Surveyor. Valve was replaced as instructed by DNV-GL surveyor and found the MGO Service tank level to be stable for the 6 hours.    Further to above MGO & MDO tanks Air vents name plates were interchanged since yard delivery.     From the calculation it has been found that approximately 2.6 M3 of MGO overflown from Air vent. Out of Which 2.4 M3 collected in 12 drums of capacity 200 litre each (12 X 0.2 M3).  And 0.2 M3 (200 Litres) remains unaccounted on board. We suspect approximately 200 Litres may have escaped overboard to cause the oil sheen on the ship side & rest has contained as solid waste,  absorbed by the saw dust and the oil absorbent pads.
DirectCause: a) Direct Cause  - Defective Valve 182V, due to which MGO Service tank gradually filled up from the return line.  b) Indirect Cause  - Inadequate Supervision. Close monitoring of Bunkered tanks was not carried out after  bunkering operation was completed. Minor Leak and Gradual increase in MGO Storage  and Service tank not identified.  - High Level alarm was not reset to a higher limit, and high-level alarm remained in active condition.  c) Root Cause  - Lack of Planning. Risk Assessment was carried out by Ship Staff and Bunkering check List was followed, however resetting of High-Level Alarm was not identified in Risk Assessment.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1. All Engineers briefed on the requirement to change the setting of the high-level alarm once activated. Chief engineers Standing order and pre bunkering meeting to include the below instructions. Company will be advised to alert other vessels.  2. All Engineers and watchkeepers briefed that in case of bunkering to 90% and above, the sounding levels of MDO/MGO service tanks to be monitored and recorded every watch. A notice to this effect will be displayed near the tank and the alarm panel. Chief Engineers standing orders updated. Company advised to notify other vessels.  3. Valve No 182V was removed and replaced in the presence of Class surveyor. The vessel will also check similar valve No 205V as a proactive measure.  4. MGO & MDO Tanks air vent name plates interchanged to the responsible tank.
Corrective Action: Onboard Engine Room Team members briefed on the requirement to change the setting of the high-level alarm once activated but not beyond 95% at any time. Approval to be obtained from Office along with Risk Assessment. Chief engineers Standing order and pre bunkering meeting to include necessary instructions. All fleet Vessel to be alerted on this incident.
Cause Analysis: Incorrect use of equipment or machinery
LessionLearnt: Factors contributing to the incident  On 18th May 2019, prior Bunkering operation, LSMGO consumption was changed from MGO Service tank to MDO Service tank.   Bunkering operations completed on 18th May 2019, as per Bunker Plan. Vessel bunkered 410.480MT of MGO in MGO Storage tank, MGO Serv tank and MDO Serv tank. Vessel bunkered up to 89% of the MGO storage tank. The high-level alarms were originally set to activate at 86%.   Upon completion of bunkering, the high-level alarm for MGO Storage tank remained in active condition, indicating alarm. The high-level alarm set point was not changed beyond 86% in alarm monitoring system after bunkering. Vessel arrived at Yantian port on 19th May 2019 at 1442Hrs LT.   Sludge Discharge operation carried out on 19th May 2019. During analysis, noted that Sludge discharge operation, did not interfere with the LSMGO pipeline system, which rules out the possibility of any Valve being mistakenly operated in the LSMGO pipeline system.  It was also verified that no Contamination of LSMGO with HFO or Vice Versa.  While analyzing the cause, the only link between MDO Storage & MDO Service tank and MGO Storage & MGO Service tank is through Valve 182V.  When this Valve was further inspected in the presence of Class Surveyor, noted that this Valve was Leaking, in closed condition when water was poured and without any pressure. Further in the presence of DNVGL Class Surveyor, this Valve was replaced with new Valve, and observed no  increase in level in MGO Service tank. This verification assisted us in concluding that the Valve(182V) leakage was the main cause of MGO Service and Storage tank filling up.
ReviewComments: Investigation report attached for reference

Vessel: D AND K YUSUF I. AL-GHANIM
IncidentDetails: At around 2200 hrs hrs LT, more than one (01) persons boarded the vessel through Forward Centre Lead and broke into Forepeak Store.
IncidentEventDetails: At around 2200 hrs hrs LT, more than one (01) persons boarded the vessel through Forward Centre Lead and broke into Forepeak Store. The store was locked at the time of the incident. At 2210 Hrs LT, deck watch keeper observed that Forepeak Store door was open.  At this time, the Deck watch keeper was at the Bridge wing for Handing over the watch. Same was reported to Duty officer and Duty officer instructed Deck watch keeper to proceed forward and investigate.   On reaching forward, Deck watch keeper observed that the door was partially closed and the padlock was on the floor. Same was reported to Bridge. Master was informed. Chief Officer and Bosun were summoned on the Bridge. A search party was organized and they were briefed about the situation.    Search team did not encountered any person / persons forward, however, foot prints were found forward confirming the boarding. Forepeak store was thoroughly checked followed by a thorough check of the Vessel.    No security breach was observed near and around accommodation. However, approx. 20 m to 50 m of Tiger rope (22 mm) was stolen from the Forepeak Store.
ImmediateAction: Thorough search of the vessel was initiated. Anti Piracy Watches doubled up. Razor wire rigged.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Approx 20 to 50 m of Tiger rope (22 mm) stolen from Forecastle.
DirectCause: Improper Deck rounds. Lack of security awareness.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Vessel to be Hardened more effectively to avoid future occurence.
Corrective Action: Crew training for Security
LessionLearnt: To be extra vigilant at ports and while at anchorage.
ReviewComments: PSCO informed through agent. Incident also reported to IMB.
ClosingNote: PSCO informed through agent. Incident also reported to IMB.

IncidentDetails: Temperature transmitter connection at the port side manifold L1 line started leaking liquid Propane during discharge.
IncidentEventDetails: 08th May 2019 / 20:12 LT - Commenced discharging Propane.  08th May 2019 / 20:54 LT - Commenced discharging Butane.  Rate of cargo was slowly increased to maximum as per terminal requirements (about 600 MT/Hr). Pressure at manifold was 5.6 Bar.  09th May 2019 / 01:00 LT - Manifold watchkeeper reported cargo leakage from Temperature trasmitter of L1 Maniold.  Chief Officer & Gas Eng. immediately inspected the leak and realised discharging needs to be stopped to rectify the leak.  01:06 LT - Stopped discharging Propane / informed loading master who was on board.  Line was drained and C/E informed. C/E located a spare transmitter of similar dimensions. The connection was opened to fit the spare transmitter. Tried to fit the spare transmitter but this too started leaking. Thereafter line drained again, and transmitter was disconnected. The connection stub piece on the manifold line was blanked. Line was pressurized / leak test carried out again.  09th May 2019 / 02:54 LT - Resumed discharging Propane.
ImmediateAction: Cargo was immediately stopped and line drained to carry out repairs.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Temperature transmitter probe was found broken inside the pocket.  Delay of 1.8 hours in cargo discharging.
DirectCause: Equipment Failure due to ageing, Lack of supervision and compliance with procedure of cargo line draining.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Please refer the attached investigation report
Corrective Action: Kindly Carry out Inspection of all other Similar transmitter fitted on Cargo line for checking the condition of stem
ClosingNote: Investigation completed,  Refer attached report.

IncidentDetails: Detail of Armed Robbery incident as follows in chronological order:  14 May 2019 1712 : Vessel All Fast at MARTISSANT Terminal PORT AU PRINCE HAITI. No Cargo operation was going on as per charterer's instructions. Vessel was maintaining strict anti piracy watches on deck due to previously reported incidents.  15 May 2019 0300-0315 LT : Two Armed robber boarded the vessel from port sea side and threaten the deck watch who was in fwd and keeping watch and escaped with two loose mooring ropes. Duty watchman reported the incident to OOW after the incident and alarrm was immediately.  0317 LT : All Crew mustered.  0320-0420 LT : Thorough search carried out on board, Apart from two fwd loose mooring ropes, rest found in order.All crew were briefed about the situation and explained to be more vigilant.  0330 LT : Terminal security watchkeeper  boarded the vessel and was apprised of the incident.  0336 LT : PFSO, agent notified  0342 LT: All parties informed.
IncidentEventDetails: Detail of Armed Robbery incident as follows in chronological order:  14 May 2019 1712 : Vessel All Fast at MARTISSANT Terminal PORT AU PRINCE HAITI. No Cargo operation was going on as per charterer's instructions. Vessel was maintaining strict anti piracy watches on deck due to previously reported incidents.  15 May 2019 0300-0315 LT : Two Armed robber boarded the vessel from port sea side and threaten the deck watch who was in fwd and keeping watch and escaped with two loose mooring ropes. Duty watchman reported the incident to OOW after the incident and alarrm was immediately.  0317 LT : All Crew mustered.  0320-0420 LT : Thorough search carried out on board, Apart from two fwd loose mooring ropes, rest found in order.All crew were briefed about the situation and explained to be more vigilant.  0330 LT : Terminal security watchkeeper  boarded the vessel and was apprised of the incident.  0336 LT : PFSO, agent notified  0342 LT: All parties informed.
ImmediateAction: Raised alarm, Crew mustered and thorough search carried out, Concern notified, No. of Person on watch increased.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: None, During Armed Robbery vessel lost two mooring ropes. This was taken out prior berthing as per pilot instruction for making fast the fwd tugs and in case of emergency.
DirectCause: a. Direct Cause: - Armed robbers boarded the vessel from the outboard side of vessel using snap hook & rope arrangement. They held the duty seaman in captivity and escaped with 2 nos. of mooring ropes kept on the forecastle deck.  b. Indirect Cause: - Lack of Information: Normally vessels seek Security information as a part of pre arrival information from the agents for the port of call. The agents in this particular case did not provide detailed information about the prevailing security situation in the port. Vessel only came to know the security situation when they witnessed ongoing conflict between terminal and local miscreants during mooring operation.  c. Root Cause: - Inadequate Security measures by Port Facility : Adequate security measures were not taken by the port. Local miscreants with arms were seen inside the terminal and were threatening the terminal staff. No Security patrol boat was available in the port to ensure safety of waters around terminal and vessel.  Moreover, terminal was observed to be surrounded by slums and due to inadequate port security, the chances of accessing terminal by locals cannot be ruled out.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Training of ship staff as per requirement of SSP. Master to obtain pre-arrival security information from local agents. Vessel security assessment to be carried out and request for security guards, if situation warrants.
Corrective Action: Send internal MEMO to all the MSI /TSI to Liaise for  arrangement of  the Security guards for vessel calling Port Au Prince Haiti.
LessionLearnt: • The Importance of heightened degree of vigilance when calling ports in a country where there is inadequate security measures. • If agents are not providing adequate information about security situation in any port, Master to recourse to other available sources of detailed information for better management.
ReviewComments: Satisfactory
ClosingNote: Shared with the fleet vessel, Uploaded on OCIMF and report sent to IMB Kualalumpur.

IncidentDetails: The ship was initially moored alongside to the tyre fender fixed on the main forward fender with the help of chains. During the course of loading the tyre fender gradually broke into pieces and the securing chain remained on the main fender. This chain chaffed between ship side and the main fender creating abrasions, indentation and peeling of ship side paint. There was no damage to the fender or any jetty installations.
IncidentEventDetails: 30 May 2019   1430 LT: Noticed tyre fender located on forward main fender getting crushed leading to securing chain rubbing between ship side and forward main fender. Vessel informed same to loading master and requested shore assistance.  1435 LT: Ship staff rigged temporary fenders to reduce abrasion between ship side and the chain.  1450 LT: Ropes for ship’s deployed fenders parted.  1510 LT: Vessel called loading master via agent and VHF to request urgent shore assistance. Vessel was informed that it will take 30 min for loading master to arrive onboard and access the situation.  1555 LT: Vessel requested terminal to stop loading.  1612 LT: Loading suspended  1630 LT: Loading Master Onboard  1630-1700 LT: Ship staff entered 3S Ballast tank to carry out an inspection and  check for any damages / indentation on ship side.  1645 LT: Shore repair boat arrived on scene to remove the chain chaffing between ship side and main fender.  1700 LT: Chain was removed and mooring were adjusted to make ship alongside on forward main fender.  1712 LT: Loading was resumed by vessel.
ImmediateAction: Loading was suspended. Inspection of ship side was carried out by ship staff.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: The ship was initially moored alongside to the tyre fender fixed on the main forward fender with the help of chains. During the course of loading the tyre fender gradually broke into pieces and the securing chain remained on the main fender. This chain chaffed between ship side and the main fender creating abrasions, indentation and peeling of ship side paint. There was no damage to the fender or any jetty installations.
DirectCause: Material damage of shore fender fittings
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Berth layout plan to be discussed during master pilot info exchange
Corrective Action: Berth layout plan to be discussed during master pilot info exchange
ClosingNote: Closed satisfactorily.

IncidentDetails: Port side bulwark stiffeners 4 pcs near accomodation entrance buckled due to contact with shore fender while berthing at dangjin.
IncidentEventDetails: 0730lt:Pob for berthing at Dangjin port berth no.6 port side alongside.  0735lt:master pilot information exchange  0846lt:tug made fast fwd and aft bollard pull 60mt.  0912lt:first line ashore and vsl in contact with shore fenders.  vsl berthed during low water where vsl was about 4 mtrs below jetty level which made contact of bulwark area with the shore fender leading to bulwark stiffeners buckling.  0930lt:pilot away.0954lt:all fast fwd and aft 4+2.1030lt:observed damage to bulwark stiffeners buckled.
ImmediateAction: Reported same to office and checked adjacent areas for any damages.Not found any additional damages.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: During berthing the vessel was coming alongside with tide at low water, as the vessel was loaded, the fenders were high and above the shipside and these fenders touched the shipside and buckled the bulwark stiffeners on deck near bunker davit on port side.
DirectCause: NIL
Proposedcorrectiveactions: buckled stiffiners to be cropped and renewed.
ClosingNote: CAR found satisfactory and closed

IncidentDetails: HFO found leaked into pipe duct space (P).
IncidentEventDetails: After routine transfer of FO, 4/E entered pipe duct space for inspection and found HFO leaked into the space. He has alerted other Engineers about the same. Upon further inspection, it was found that 2 flexible couplings of HFO line (P) was leaking from gaskets which caused the spillage. 22 compartments were affected with an estimated quantity of 1.5 cubM HFO spillage.
ImmediateAction: Port side HFO bunker line stripped and isolated. Collection of spillage and cleaning of the space started. Informed Company and requested for arranging more cleaning materials. All flexible couplings integrity checked. Gaskets of the damaged couplings replaced.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 22 compartments of Pipe Duct space (P) was affected by about 1.5 cubM of HFO spillage. 2 of the flexible couplings' gaskets damaged. Wastage of considerable amount of manpower and cleaning materials.
DirectCause: Flexible couplings in the FO line (gaskets of which may get damaged due to temperature changes), weather changes and ship movements.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Check all pipe coupling gaskets and change as required
Corrective Action: 1. Remote montoring system for identifying any leakage from flexible couplings.2. Daily inspection and inspection after every FO transfer of pipe duct space for early identification of any leakage.
ReviewComments: Warning placcard to inspect the pipe duct every day posted near the entrance.
ClosingNote: All CE's are briefed reg inspection on daily basis

IncidentDetails: While disconnection of the hoses at SBM#2, Sriracha Port HOse Handling crane stopped working. Vessel disconnected one hose and lowered in the water using both boom and runner wire movement. After lowering one hose in the water vsl was unable to pickup the runner wire
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel disconnected one hose and lowered in the water using both boom and runner wire movement. After lowering one hose in the water vsl was unable to pickup the runner wire

IncidentEventDetails: A ) ON 07-JUN-2019 AT 1454 HRS ' FIRE PREWARNING ' RECEIVED IN ECR ALARM MONITORING PANEL  B) AT 1458 HRS FIRE ALARM PANEL CHECKED AND FOUND MAIN DECK INCINERATOR ROOM SMOKE SENSOR WAS ACTIVATED  C) AT 1504 HRS FOURTH ENGINEER RUSHED TO THE SPOT AND NOTED SMOKE WAS COMING OUT FROM INCINERATOR ROOM   D) AT 1510 GENERAL ALARM SOUNDED BY FOURTH ENGG BY ACTIVATING MANUAL CALL POINT NEAR ACC A/C ROOM   E) AT 1512 ALL CREW MUSTERED   F) AT 1514 FIRE FIGHTING TEAM ENTERED THE COMPARTMENT AND EXTINGUISHED THE FIRE BY PORTABLE DCP EXTINGUISHER  G) AT 1518 FIRE TEAM RETURNED FROM THE COMPARTMENT AFTER EXTINGUISHED  H) AT 1522 BACK UP TEAM ENTERED THE COMPARTMENT TO ASSESS THE SITUATION FOR RE-IGNITION   I) AT 1530 BACK UP TEAM RETURNED FROM THE COMPARTMENT AND REPORTED NO SIGN OF RE-IGNITION AND REPORTED FIRE HAS BEEN COMPLETELY CONTAINED  J) AT 1534 ALL INCINERATOR ASH BAGS REMOVED FROM THE PREMISES  K) AT 1550 WHOLE INCINERATOR COMPARTMENT CLEANED AND FIRE WATCH POSTED

Found that the vessel is yawing extremely and not holding position satisfactorily  despite several manouvers. IncidentDetails: Vessel sailed out from Muhammad Bin Qasim(Pakistan) Container terminal QICT no.9 on 08th March 2019 / 1254Hrs Lt.  Vessel ran aground in the Bin Qasim channel at 1510Hrs Lt. with two Pilots onboard and en-route to next port Nhava Sheva (India). Reported to Port Authorities, Vessel Managers, Owners, Flag State Authorities and Charterers.  On board Pilots immediately ordered four Tugs from the port authorities to assist Vessel to refloat. Vessel refloated at 2124 Hrs Lt /08th March 2019, after around 6hrs. The channel was clear for traffic during this period with no pollution & damage to own vessel.  Vessel continued her passage to Next port Nhava Sheva after verifying all Ballast and Bunker Tank Soundings.  Vessel arrived next port Nhava Sheva on 10th March 2019 / 0730Hrs Lt, but Under Water Inspection was not possible due to the quality of water. Vessel arrived port Mundra on 12th March 2019 / 2130Hrs Lt, where Under Water Inspection was carried out, and noted that no damage to the Hull, Propeller and Rudder. Ballast tank Inspection carried out and observed to be in good order.
IncidentEventDetails: On 07th March 2019 / 0954Hrs Lt, Vessel arrived at port Muhammad Bin Qasim, Container Terminal QICT no.9, for Cargo Operations. On 08th March 2019 / 1146Hrs Lt, two pilots boarded Vessel for sailing from port, and Master/Pilot information exchange was carried out as per Pilot Card (OP02) and acknowledged by both pilots. On completion of Cargo operation at 1218Hrs Lt, Vessel completed the Pre-Departure checks and was on Standby at 1224Hrs Lt, awaiting clearance from port authorities for one Tanker Vessel to pass ahead of Own Vessel. On Clearance from port authorities, Own vessel casted off all lines at 1254Hrs Lt.   Pilot onboard conveyed to Master after discussing with the inbound Vessel(Navios Unite) pilot, that own Vessel will pass the inbound Vessel port to port at  buoy 10 and 11. Pilot also informed Ship Staff that the Width of the channel between buoy 10 and 11 was about 0.2Nm, whereas at the Turn the Channel was about 0.3NM wide and after the turn the width of the channel south of buoy 7 and 8 was about 0.107 Nm.   Own vessel pilot informed that, vessel has to clear the channel before 1530Hrs Lt due to ebbing tide and hence was asking the other Vessel pilot either to come in early or wait outside the Channel.  At 1500Hrs Lt, Own Vessel Passed Buoy ‘10’ and ‘11’, however the incoming vessel did not pass at the agreed meeting point and vessel had to pass each other in the Vicinity of buoy no.9.   Vessel was proceeding at about 9.4 kts and experiencing Strong Ebb tide. The inbound vessel was making a speed of about 13.5Kts and drifting towards Buoy 8A.   After passing the inbound Vessel, Own vessel Pilot ordered full ahead at 1508Hrs Lt, in order to make a effective turn to port side to counter the strong ebbing tide.    Further Vessel had to alter about 40 deg  stbd to enter between buoy 7(BEACON 6) and 8.  Vessel had increased to 52 rpm to get more speed to improve steerage and counter the tide effect and to make a Stbd turn, however Vessels turn to Stbd was not sufficient enough to maintain the Vessel in Channel as the drift due to lateral speed was high.  Vessel eventually touched bottom at 1512Hrs Lt on the port side with the speed instantaneously dropping to Zero and further the vessel developed a Stbd list of around 5 degrees.
ImmediateAction: Harbormaster communicated and tugs arranged for assistance. All tanks and void spaces inspected. Vessel arrived next port Nhava Sheva on 10th March 2019 / 0730Hrs Lt, but Under Water Inspection was not possible due to the quality of water. Vessel arrived port Mundra on 12th March 2019 / 2130Hrs Lt, where Under Water Inspection was carried out, and noted that no damage to the Hull, Propeller and Rudder. Ballast tank Inspection carried out and observed to be in good order.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: The vessel ran aground in Port qasim channel while outbound. As per the underwater inspection carried out at port of Mundra,India, there were no apparent damages noticed on the hull.
DirectCause: While passing the channel own vessel passed the inbound ‘NAVIOS UNITE’ port to port as agreed. However, the inbound vessel that was supposed to be on the southern side of the channel, came closer to the center of the channel causing own vessel to go northern limit of the channel to avoid interaction/close quarter situation. After passing the inbound vessel, own vessel was unable to return to the center of the channel, causing own vessel to run aground outside the channel before completing the next turn. The progress of the plan as decided through master pilot exchange was not monitored adequately. The initial plan for passing near buoy 10/11 was altered due to late entry of the inbound vessel in the channel. This change was not reviewed, and suitable action was not initiated in ample time.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: a. Alert regarding this incident in Bin Qasim channel to be shared with fleet vessels highlighting the dangers & precautions necessary. b. Details of this incident to be shared with inhouse training team for using this as case study during BTM and Large vessel handling courses. c. Campaign to be initiated for effective ‘Master Pilot Info Exchange’ and periodic review of the same while under pilotage. d. Debriefing of bridge team members involved in this incident. e.     Campaign to be initiated for effective ‘Master Pilot Info Exchange’, periodic review of  the same while under pilotage and ‘Dangers of Interaction’ with reference to  MGN199(M).  f) Internal Audit to be Carried out, when Vessel at Sea to audit effectiveness of SMS, and  address any concerns observed during the Audit process. g) Onboard Ship Staff to be briefed regarding this incident, along with Suitable case  studies, to ensure close monitoring of actions by Pilot, and effective communication
Corrective Action: b.Details of this incident to be shared with inhouse training team for using this as case study during BTM and Large vessel handling courses.
LessionLearnt: The master pilot exchange must include the details of points where the vessel cannot pass another vessel in the narrow part of channel and contingencies discussed  with all officers.
ClosingNote: All corrective actions in place and master incl. all officers debriefed for the incident while the internal audit. The post-incident underwater inspection confirmed that there was no apparent damage to the hull. Training carried out on all vessels in the fleet for precautions in narrow channels for transit & Pilot master information exchange.

IncidentDetails: Aft Spring line struck in Mooring Boat propellor, while berthing.
IncidentEventDetails: During Master & Pilot exchange of information it was agreed to use heaving line for passing the spring lines (fwd & aft 2 at a time) & rest lines with the mooring boat, as the on shore wind was quite strong & safety of the mooring boat was a concern.  While coming along side to the jetty, terminal insisted to use mooring boats for the spring lines.  Vessel passed the aft spring lines via the mooring boat and the lines were slacked as per boat instruction.  After passing the tail rope of the spring aft on the hook mooring boat let go all the coiled rope on the boat & in order to manoeuvre herself used her engines, which lead one of the aft spring line getting entangelled in the boat’s propellor.
ImmediateAction: Full length of mooring rope was lowered to the mooring boat & she was towed by the mooring boat in FWD station to her dock.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Minor damage of the aft spring line
DirectCause: 1) Unfavourable weather. Strong onshore winds.  2) Low lighting at the work place. ( Between ship sde and the berth fenders)  3) Wrong handling of mooring ropes by the boat man.  4) Manoeuvere of the boat while the coil of mooring line was still floating near the boats propellor.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Use heaving line instead using of the mooring boat, incase of weather constraints to use mooring boat.  Duty officer to be more vigilant while passing the ropes.
LessionLearnt: The mooring ropes should be released carefully by the boat so that it does not  foul the propeller.
ClosingNote: Noted the proposed corrective action. Mooring rope changed end to end as it suffered damage to one of its sttrand

IncidentDetails: At Antwerp,1 hr notice for  departure was received at 15:00 hrs. At 16:00 hrs standby engines was given and  vessel  was running 3 AE’s during that time (1,2 &4).#1 &#4 was on “gravity” line and #2 & #3 was on main line. Suddenly #4 AE got tripped and subsequently #1 &#2 tripped resulting in a complete black  out. Vessel delayed due to blackout of running AE's
IncidentEventDetails: 15:00 hrs : 1 hour notice for ME given to Engine room  15:10 hrs : 30 min notice for BT  15:15 hrs : Telegraph test and blow through carried out  15:20 hrs : All bridge control tested including steering gear test  15:45 hrs : Pilot onboard  15:47 hrs : BT tried out and found satisfactory  15:52 hrs : Master pilot information exchanged  16:00 hrs : SBE/control on bridge  16:05 hrs : Shore gangway cleared and ME tried out .Instructed Fwd and Aft stations to pick up tug line.  16:06 hrs : All bridge alarm activated and power went off ,total blackout.  16:08 hrs : Fwd and Aft mooring stations winch power lost and bridge ordered not to pick up tug line.  16:10 hrs : ER confirmed all 3 AE's tripped (1,2 &4 were running onload)  16:15 hrs : Agent informed about the situation  16:18 hrs : Tried to start no:1 AE  16:24 hrs : Synergy office was informed  16:25 hrs : #1 AE was started   16:30 hrs : Power restored by #1 AE which was onload  16:34 hrs : Tried to start #2 AE   16:38 hrs : Started #2 AE and on load  16:38 hrs : Pilot informed that in 10 minutes remaining vessel needs to start or else will not be able to sail as will miss tidal window.  16:40 hrs : #1 AE & #2 AE are running and onload.  16:42 hrs : BT not available  16:45 hrs : System failure alarm and imperfect w/h condition alarm activated.  16:52 hrs : pilot informed master as vessel running out of time and it is impossible now and not safe to sail.  16:54 :ME control on bridge and tugs standby  16:56 : Pliot declined sailing and told vessel will not match with tidal window and informed VTS &all party.  16:58 hrs : Pilot cancelled tug and informed vessel that within 30 minutes it's impossible to turn the ship and sail  17:12 hrs : Pilot away  17:18 hrs : All ME alarms reset and ME tried out from bridge control and found satisfactory.  17:20 hrs : Agent was informed and confirmed pilot booked for 02 : 00 hrs for 07/June/19  17:30 hrs : #4 AE started  17:32 hrs : BT tried out and found satisfactory.  17:32 hrs : Antwerp coordination center was informed that vessel is ready to sail and got reply ailing confirmed at 0200 hrs /07
ImmediateAction: Tried to restart #4 but it was not starting .Suspected airlock ,#1 AE was purged and successfully started and taken  onload. Power was restored and #2 AE was started and taken on load. Tried to start the ME but it was not starting .Upon investigation ,found “Actuator abnormal” alarm was activated. Found the fuse inside the servo unit panel blown off. Fuse was replaced and alarm was reset.ME was tried out on “Ahead” and “Astern” and found satisfactory.AE's were put back in operation and power restored
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: VESSEL DELAYED FOR DEPARTURE.MISSED THE TIDAL   WINDOW AND STAYED ALONGSIDE AT BERTH  FOR 10 HRS WAITING FOR THE NEXT TIDAL WINDOW.
DirectCause: #4 & #1 AE's tripped followed by tripping of #1 AE while cleaning the filters.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1.Any unplanned job should be strictly brought forward to the notice of Chief Engineer.  2.Proper risk assessment should be carried out prior commencement of any task.  3.Vessel should avoid any critical task during critical operations like berthing,unberthing and manoeuvring.  4.Senior management should carry out proper training to all the staff before commencement of any task.  5.Supervision from the ER management team is required at all times during all critical task in ER.  6.Onboard training for all the critical  task should be carried out very frequently and record of the same to be maintained in SF 17 .  7.Vessel planning to make a “ship specific” manoeuvring checklist for all the AE’s and all the associated filters.  8.Ship specific cleaning schedule has been prepared for filters associated with ME,AE & Blr. All the ER staff have been briefed about the filter cleaning schedule.Record of the same will be maintained and updated onboard.  9.Every hour,running AE parameters will be monitored by the duty engineer and condition of the same to be entered in “Remarks” column in the ER log book.  10.Critical task should not be carried out during critical operations like unberthing,berthing and manoeuvring

IncidentDetails: While proceeding to Sabine Pilot station, E/Room informed that Main engine unit no 5 not firing.  On further investigation it was found that UNit no 5 exhaust valve was not fully closing.
IncidentEventDetails: 10 Jun 2019  =========  0930: 1 Hr notice to E/Room  0930-1013: All controls tested as per Bridge Checklist 7 (OP09F)  1015: Con wth Master, BWL-2  1020: Main Engines tested/tried out ahead/astern  1030: SBE/Commenced heaving Port anchor  1048: Anchor Aweigh  1048: Vessel procceding to pilot station  1203: Due to problem in M/engine, M/Engine stopped  1203: Start investigating M/engine issue  1220: Vessel informed Sabine Pilot on VHF ch 14 that vessel is aborting pilot boarding and procceding to Sabine East anchorage  1224: Synergy Office Tech Supdt Mr Kunal was informed by telcon about Main Engine problem and vessel aborted Pilot boarding  1229: BW operations Capt Siddharaj was informed by telcon about same  1235: Promar agency was informed by telcon and requested to inform all concerned parties including charterers  1239: Owners agent Bulbous was informed by telcon and requested to inform USCG about the same  1336: Vessel safely anchored at Sabine East anchorage area  1354: Anchor brought up and Main engines stopped/FWE  1354: Started Repair works on Main engine by ship staff
ImmediateAction: Vessel informed Sabine Pilot on VHF ch 14 that vessel is aborting pilot boarding and procceding to Sabine East anchorage. Vessel informed Technical managers, Owners, USCG via agent, Charterer's Agent.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Main Engine Unit No 5 Exhaust valve was not fully closing, due to which main Engine unit no 5 was not firing.
DirectCause: After dismantling the exhaust valve observed that the air was leaking from the air cylinder and air piston which would have resulted in exhaust valve not closing fully. The reasons for leakage between air cylinder and air piston could be  1. Deterioration of sealing ring and guide ring due to age factor.  2. Spare part used could be of poor quality and not from maker.  3. Water in the exhaust spring air.   4. Low exhaust valve spring air pressure
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1. Only original spares from maker to be used for overhauling the exhaust valve.  2. M/E control air, spring air and safety air to drain frequently to ensure no water going inside.  3. Performance of air dryer to be checked and spares to order if required.  4. M/E all unit exhaust valves to check for proper closing while running and before maneuvering.   5. Lowering of running hour for overhaul of M/E exhaust valve to be discussed with vessel Supdtt.   6. One fully assembled and tested exhaust valve to be ready for mounting all the time in case of emergency.   7. Spring air pressure increased from 6.5 to 7.0 bar.
ClosingNote: Exhaust valve renewed and main engine tried out satisfactory

IncidentDetails: DAMAGE OF LDCL( LOAD DEPENDENT CYLINDER LINER) PUMP SUCTION PRESSURE GAUGE DAMAGED.
IncidentEventDetails: AT 0730HRS DUTY MOTOR MAN NOTICED LEAKAGE FROM LDCL PUMP SUCTION PRESSURE GAUGE, SAME WAS REPORTED TO 2ND ENGG ON DUTY AND TO CE AT 0731 HRS .  AT 0734 HRS LT AFTER ASSESMENT OF THE SITUTION AT 0740 HRS IT WAS INFORMED TO MASTER AND BRIDGE THAT THE ASSESMENT IS UNDER PROGRESS .   AT 0740 HRS LT CE ADVICE MNASTER THAT IT IS BEST TO ARREST THE LEAK AT THIS STAGE ITSLEF.
ImmediateAction: 0745 MASTER ASSESSED THE SITUATION AND THE TRAFFIC DENSITY/ PROXIMITY OF NAVIGATIONAL HAZARDS IN THE VISCINTY . SITUATIONAL RISK WAS EVALUATED AND DECISION WAS MADE TO STOP THE VESSEL AND CARRY OUT THE REPAIRS.  0750 SLOW DOWN INTIATED .  0805 NAVIGATIONAL WARNING WAS BROADCASTED ON VHF CHANNEL 16 AND AIS STATUS CHANGED TO " NOT UNDER COMMAND " , NAVIGATIONAL SHAPES DISPLAYED.  0812 ME BROUGHT TO COMPLETE STOP  0815-0850 A NEW PRESSURE GAUGE WAS INSTALLED WITH AN ISOLATION VALVE.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Vessel was enroute in English Channel on a voyage from Rotterdam to USG for orders.  At about 0730 hrs LT on 17th May 2019, the duty motorman observed cooling water leaking from the LDCL (Load dependent Cylinder liner jacket) pump inlet pressure gauge.  The gauge glass had bulged out and the water was pouring out of the pressure gauge dial at an estimated rate of about 100 ltrs/hr. Duty engineer was informed and same was immediately conveyed to C/E. A quick assessment lead to a decision to stop the M/E and rectify the leak soonest as the pressure on the suction side was about 4.5 bar. Master was informed and a quick risk assessment was performed reviewing the prevailing conditions including location, weather, traffic and estimated time required for repairs, after which Master permitted to stop the M/E and complete the repairs.    The M/E was stopped to carry out the repair. A new pressure gauge was installed along with an isolation valve.
DirectCause: a. Direct Cause: - Equipm ent Failure – Bourdon tube failure leading to water leakage from the pressure gauge.  b. Indirect Cause: - Failure o f Pressure gauge: Due to material failure of pressure gauge assembly, water started gushing in due to which gauge glass gave away causing water leak. c. Root Cause: - Inadequ ate Material standard – Material of pressure gauge assembly failed causing build up of pressure inside gauge casing causing bursting of gauge glass.  Inadequate design: No Isolation valve was provided with the pressure gauge. This would have allowed isolation of a leaking pressure gauge and could have prevented the unplanned stoppage.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1-Installation of isolation valves to pressure gauge.  2-Spare isolation valve / pressure gauge to be maintained on board.
Corrective Action: Installation of isolation valves to pressure gauge
LessionLearnt: Inadequate design can lead to failure of important machineries on board.  It is responsibility of ship staff to identify such machinery with inadequate design and action plan to be discussed with the office for rectification in order to avoid any untoward incident.
ReviewComments: Satisfactory
ClosingNote: Incident shared with the fleet vessels and Checked on sister vessels and other vessels with similar arrangements. Isolation valve was installed on vessels where it was not fitted.

IncidentDetails: OIL SOAKED RAG SELF IGNITED DUE TO SUNLIGHT & HIGH DECK TEMPERATURE IN SUMMER MONTH.
IncidentEventDetails: 1030 Hrs LT : During tool box meeting with deck dept it was decided to prepare stbd Manifold reducer for Discharging  1105 Hrs LT : while disconnecting reducers it was observed that there was some cargo of Soyabean oil in No.2 Manifold pipe. Same was cleaned using a synthetic material rag. After cleaning the rag was kept on the maindeck and deck crew started changing reducer for No.5 & No.6 Manifold.  1115 Hrs LT : Pumpman on deck smelled smoke & upon investigation it was found that oil soaked rag kept on deck was burning due to strong sunlight & hot deck steel plates.
ImmediateAction: Fire was extinguished immediatly by water from nearest fres water hydrant.  Incident disscussed with all crew.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: NO DAMAGE / INJURY
DirectCause: HOT STEEL SURFACES DUE TO STRONG SUNLIGHT IN SUMMER MONTH AT MUMBAI ANCHORAGE
Proposedcorrectiveactions: ISSUE DISCUSSED WITH ALL PERSONNEL ONBOARD EXPLAINED THE CONSEQUENCES.
Corrective Action: please discuss with the entire ship team that the oil soaked rags should not be kept on warm pipelines but only in thrie designated buckets.
ClosingNote: it is extremely important that oil soaked rags should be kept only in neoprene buckets.

IncidentDetails: Vessel is proceeding to San Francisco (CA -USA ) with Max Safe speed after loading Cargo-Alkylate  at second load port -Sendai Japan as per the Charterer instructions .  Vessel is  following the weather routeing as advised by WNI. Based on latest forecast provided by WNI , Vessel is on  south route - RL via 34N/144E to 34/160W for passing the south of low pressure system and avoid over 6m severe weather . Weather Forecast well discussed & Heavy weather precaution taken. Max RPM reduced  adjusted to 103-106 to avoid overloading of ME & Ship's Hull in present weather condition  Vessel experienced rough sea & Heavy swell coming from 6 point ABAFT of Port beam .  it was observed  that there were occassionally  heavy spray of seas near & abreast of  the 1 Port of water Ballast tanks and vessel was rolling easily upto 15 Degree.  As the weather improved today morning (28-March -2019 ) for time being .Round were taken on Deck .  on Further investigation , have noticed following damages to Port Side Gangway .  1. Gangway is stowed in vertical position  as per the design  & secured as required , Total length of Gangway - 15.15 Mtrs , There is vertical bend of 3-4 CM appx  along the length and maximum at 9 steps from top . Gangway is having Total 42 steps .  2. 02Nos of Stowing bolts for Gangway  were bent severely on 2 places and broken at another two places .  3. Lashing eye/Pads on Gangway for attaching the Securing bolts  were bent at one location & bent+cracked at the other location.   4. Back plates attached to these 9 Steps from top of gangway has crack  on upperside of back plate appx  1-3 cm at on one end  & bent at one end. Width of back Plate - 15 CM,
