IncidentDetails: Damged to Scrubber Gas passsage packing and demsiter.
IncidentEventDetails: During Galveston lightering discharge operation while starting IG  system tripped on High  gas outlet temperature (Setting at 65’C) and temperature went up to 70’C .Again checked with starting other blower and second one also tripped on high gas outlet temperature. On further checks Scrubber cooling water pump has lost suction due to Sea chest filter choked due to jelly fish invasion. Scrubber cooling water given through main fire pump for the mean time and Pressure adjusted to 0.1 MPa (which is normally maintained during operations). Sea chest filters cleaned and resumed IG tower cooling with Scrubber pump.  AFter discharge operation inspected Scrubber tower on 18 June 2019. Found Gas passage packing stack completley collapsed and demsiter damaged due to overheating. Scrubber non flow alarm which did not activate when scrubber pump lost suction.
ImmediateAction: New spares ordered. and ordered spares temprorary repairs done till vessel receives orginal spares
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: AFter discharge operation inspected Scrubber tower on 18 June 2019. Found Gas passage packing stack completley collapsed and demsiter damaged due to overheating. Scrubber non flow alarm which did not activate when scrubber pump lost suction.
DirectCause: Scrubber non flow alarm which did not activate when scrubber pump lost suction.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Non flow alarm Switch float clened and boxed back. Function checked working. New spares for Gas stackings and Demister requested in Shi palm
Corrective Action: Non flow alarm Switch float clened and boxed back. Function checked working. New spares for Gas stackings and Demister requested in Shi palm
ClosingNote: Critical alarms to be checked regularly and definitely prior the operation and spares to be maintained always. Learning to be circulated among all concerned

IncidentDetails: At around 1654LT on 19-June-2019, suddenly a Fire alarm was activated from manual call point.   Upon further information, it was observed that the burning soot from the M/E Exhaust caused fire on polypropelene polyester tug rope stowed on top of fire station (STBD Engine casing).  Immediately the crew was mustered and emergency fire team extinguished the fire using fire hoses including the boudary cooling from inside the E/R casing bulkhead.
IncidentEventDetails: 1616 LT – Commenced Increasing  RPM for soot blow  1652LT – Observed Smoke from top of stbd side funnel deck (Above fire station)   1654 LT - Fire Alarm activated from upp deck stbd manual call point by one of the crew member.  1654 LT – PA Announcement   1655 LT – Engines Half a head   1655 LT – Master on bridge/ Contingency checklist ‘CN-03-Fire and Explosion’ referred.  1655  LT – All mustered   1657 LT -  Fire Pump started  1657LT -  Rigged fire hoses  1658 LT - Sprinkler stby for paint store /Boundary cooling for funnel/Emerg’cy generator room  1658 LT-  Commenced Fire Fighting   1720 LT – Fire extinguished   1720 LT – Informed Office thru 24 hrs emergency contact number.  1720 to 1735 LT – Checked for hot spots in  fire station, E/R , Funnel , Blower room , Paint store , Deck stores , P/R , Fire station ,garbage area, Strng Gr RM  All found ok .
ImmediateAction: 1. All ropes removed from the poop deck and checks carried out for any other combustible material.  2. EGB soot blow again carried out in order to check for any further burning soot particles/lumps. No burning soot observed.  3. Fire watch for any re-occurrence or any hot spots were carried out for inside E/R and poop deck. All found in order.  4. All duty Engg. And OOW briefed for being extra cautious during next soot blow operations.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1. All crew training to be carried out against stowage of any combustible material near funnel areas or poop deck.  2. During Economizer soot blow OOW on bridge to keep thorough watch around the funnel deck
Cause Analysis: Failure to follow rules and regulations

IncidentDetails: Port side monorail crane wire parted during securing of monorail hook by inching process to its stowage position.
IncidentEventDetails: The hook was aligned vertical to its parking position and hoisted further up to upper limit position (approx. 1m from the final stowage position).After reaching the upper limit , parking of hooks was done by  operating local switch which moves the block at a slow speed.    During inching operation the block was not exactly aligned to its locking position and was further adjusted by inching which caused additional strain on the wire and as a result the wire parted. Cut off limit switch not set in correct position. Fortunately the hook was still in partially locked position and did not fall on deck.
ImmediateAction: Additional lashing of the hook was taken to prevent it from falling down on deck and cause any damage/injury. Vessel has replaced the parted wire with new spare wire rope and the hook has been secured back in position.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Port side monorail crane wire parted during securing of monorail hook by inching process to its stowage position and No injury.
DirectCause: 1)Cut off limit switch not set in correct position.  2)Block was not aligned in position and inching operation was carried out which caused additional strain on wire.  3)Inching process by local operation switch moved the block very fast.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1.Crew to be well trained  on correct operation procedure while securing/inching.  2.Cut off limit to be checked prior commence of  inching operation.  3.Inching speed of motor  need to be checked before every use of operation.
Corrective Action: To review status onboard all Sister Vessels and implement corrective action by familiarizing on correct procedure and additional precautions to be taken to be included
ClosingNote: Reviewed process of Securing Monorail and implemented corrective action

IncidentDetails: On 17th June 2019, at around 07:55 hrs (LT), when vessel was exiting Hormuz strait there was huge fluctuation of generator frequency. And subsequently alarm came for "440 V low frequency" and immediately after that generators tripped and vessel suffered black out.
IncidentEventDetails: Initial investigation by 2nd engineer revealed water in duplex filter of AE supply pump (line is directly from HFO service tank).   For general check when bottom drain for duplex filter (AE supply pump) was opened, clean water was visible for few secs. Later when HFO service tank was drained, found sizeable amount of water.  HFO settling tank was also having good amount of water, which was evident while draining the tank.  Factors leading to water ingress:  ? Vessel was rolling considerably from 11th June to 16th June 2019, which would have worsened the tank’s condition.  ? On 11th & 14th June 2019, bunker tank (2C, 3P) stripped to segregate tanks for taking fresh bunker intake at Fujairah.  Possibility of water ingress through stripping of tanks could be a probable cause.    There is a suspect of purifier malfunction. So sample has been sent to lab for analysis.
ImmediateAction: Power was immediately restored within 45 secs by Emergency generator and within no time Aux engine #3 (which is normaly kept on DO for starting) started and taken on load and all sea load was restored within 5 mins.      Thorough draining of water from Aux engine supply pump duplex filter line & HFO service and settlign carried out. Gradually after draining water, both affected generators were started on DO and shifted to HFO and load shared.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: No damage to machineries / injury to personnel reported
DirectCause: Water in fuel was the main reason for black out.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Proper watch keeping routines of draining water from HFO tanks to be followed strictly.  Possibility of purifier malfunctioning cannot be rulled out, so sample of HFO purifier #1 & #2 sent for lab analysis.
Corrective Action: Training to be imparted to all watch keepers regd. the importance of draining HFO settling and service tanks
ReviewComments: Noted corrective action taken immediately and power restored. Purifier efficiency to be  ascertained once we receive the the lab analysis reports.
ClosingNote: Vessel to strictly adhere to basic watch keeping routines in order to avoid machinery failures

IncidentDetails: 1) 2 head lines tail rope parted.  2) 2 forward spring tail rope damage.(1 strand on each cut)  3) 1 aft breast tail rope damage (1 strand- Chaffing damage).  4) 1 Stern line tail rope damage. (1 strand cut)
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel is doing multiple STS operation off Lome, Togo.  During the Fourth STS operation with MT SKS Darent.  Own ship was daughter vessel with 4 head lines and 2 fwd spring & 4 stern lines and 2 aft spring  Mother vessel (SKS Darent) with 2 head line and 2 fwd spring & 2 stern lines & 2 aft spring.  The weather turned bad around 2000 hrs and at 2012 hrs- V/L is ready to discharge cargo, and awaiting MT SKS Darent readiness, when the deck crew reported, that the head line had parted.  On  checking forward, found that 2 headlines had parted, and the bow of both the ship had moved apart and there was weight again on the remaining 2 lines and were rendering continuously, the head line was continously monitored and plans made for casting off.  All information passed on to bridge and decided to stop operation and cast off.  IG stopped, COPs stopped and hoses disconnected.  All lines cast off after great difficulty.
ImmediateAction: Stopped all operations, Cargo hoses disconnected, & vessel cast off.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1) 2 head lines tail rope parted.  2) 2 forward spring tail rope damage.(1 strand on each cut)  3) 1 aft breast tail rope damage (1 strand- Chaffing damage).  4) 1 Stern line tail rope damage. (1 strand cut)
DirectCause: # Lack Of Planning : Adverse weather condition was anticipated, but seeing that it could be short operation of few hours, it was decided to go ahead with the operation. Similar sized vessel used for the operation, which do not allow proper mooring to be carried out, and both with mooring wire and tail which does not allow for mooring rope elongation during excessive roll of the vessel.. # Lack of Compliance : Despite several request by the mooring master and MT Bryanston, MT SKS Darent kept all their lines slack during the entire operation, leading to all the weight being transferred to our mooring lines, which ended up parting our lines
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Vessel not to carryout STS operation under any commercial pressure from chartering team/ mooring master, STS to be avoided if adverse weather is expected .  A good risk assessment should be prepared if STS between similar sized vessels is planned. All hazards like use of similar kind of mooring lines and from same leads should be identified and mitigated as required.
Corrective Action: Vessel not to carryout STS operation under any commercial pressure from chartering team/ mooring master, STS to be avoided if adverse weather is expected .  A good risk assessment should be prepared if STS between similar sized vessels is planned. All hazards like use of similar kind of mooring lines and from same leads should be identified and mitigated as required.
ClosingNote: Job closed 18/07

IncidentDetails: The Vessel At Anchor at General Santos Philipppines.  On 1240H LT 11th June 2019   The Tugboat of  Embarking Authorities approached the vessel on its Port Side  and  Hit the Pilot Ladder that leading to its  Damage.
IncidentEventDetails: 1230H Tugboat Approached to the vessel at   Anchorage Area  1240H Tugboat Alongside and Hit Port Side Pilot Ladder
ImmediateAction: Duty A/B immediately  Reported the Incident to the Duty Officer and Carried out investigation why the Pilot Ladder was  Hit and Damaged by the Tugboat.
DirectCause: Duty Able seaman Unable to Secured Pilot Ladder before the Tugboat Alongside to the Vessel  and the  lock of communication to the Tugboat.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: All Deck Crew Carried out Training on How to Avoid Damage to the Pilot Ladder.
Corrective Action: All Deck Crew Carried out Training on How to Avoid Damage to the Pilot Ladder.
ClosingNote: To be discussed in safety meeting and regular monitoring while rigging of pilot ladder / boat approaching ladder / well clear from ship.

IncidentDetails: Vessel was at Pemex espogen north terminal, Manzanillo with mooring pattern 2+3+2 Forward & 2+2+2 Aft.There was not enough lead in spring lines. Vessel was idle at port awaiting terminal readiness to start next parcel discharging.Vessel was experiencing  surging and aft spring mooring line parted due to chafting despite of putting three layers of canvas cover over the mooring ropes and regular application of grease on the fair leads.
IncidentEventDetails: 1.Vessel was at Pemex espogen north terminal, Manzanillo. Vessel was experiencing heavy surging   2. One additional breast line passed in forward as an additional precautionary measure.  3. vessel placed additional 3 layers canvas covers over mooring ropes and regularly applied grease on the fairleads.  4. One of the aft spring rope parted at 0525 LT
ImmediateAction: Immediately informed terminal and passed 2 extra loose ropes as aft springs and made an eye on the parted roped and passed.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: One aft spring line parted & another spring chafed moderately.
DirectCause: 1.Heavy surging  2.Stern lines was almost having lead of breast lines.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Vessel to cast off in case of bad weather is anticipated.
Corrective Action: 1. Anti chafting mats to be ordered. 2. Vessel to cast off incase of expected bad weather.
Cause Analysis: Incorrect use of equipment or machinery
ClosingNote: Checked and closed satisfactorily.

IncidentDetails: 3 Mooring ropes parted and 3 Mooring rope experienced severe damage.
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel berthed at MLT berth On 16th Jun 2019 at 1148LT Hrs with 3+2+2 mooring configuration forward and aft as per pilot instruction.  Even though Break water is established for Terminal it is not sufficient for SW wind and swell.  During stay at MLT berth Vessel damaged 6 ropes (3 ropes parted and 3 ropes severely damaged). Vessel taken all the precaution to reduce chaffing by applying grease regularly but surging at berth was excessive due to long swell.
ImmediateAction: Additional loose ropes passed and secured on bollards.  Tug Employed
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 3 Mooring ropes -  FWD & AFT spring and Headline parted. 3 Mooring ropes  FWD & AFT spring severly damaged.
DirectCause: Excessive surging at berth
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Tugs to be stand by at all times during cargo operation in such weather condition.
LessionLearnt: Vessel to put canvas socks on the ropes near fairleads and regular lubrication of ropes while at berth.
ClosingNote: Vessel to put canvas socks on the ropes near fairleads and regular lubrication of ropes while at berth.

IncidentDetails: At/around : 1330LT/28June2019  Vsl commenced discharging RBD Palm oil cargo from tanks 6P & 6S via 7S Manifold,  as per agreement with the terminal.  Duty AB ( Kavrendar ) confirmed cargo flow at manifold and duty office in CCR (2Off) confirmed cargo flowing out from 6P and 6S CT as ullages for both tanks were found increasing.  At/around : 1340LT/28June2019  Manifold pressure increased to 4bar on terminal request at 7S Manifold.  At/Around : 1343LT/28June2019  Second officer reported that 7S CT pressure is found rising sharpely and asked duty crew to activate the PV valve manually.  At/around : 1345LT/28June2019  as OS Akash deep rushed to activate the PV valve cargo RBD Palm Oil spilled from 7S CT PV valve due to high pressure in tank.
IncidentEventDetails: At/around : 1330LT/28June2019  Vsl commenced discharging RBD Palm oil cargo from tanks 6P & 6S via 7S Manifold,  as per agreement with the terminal.  Duty AB ( Kavrendar ) confirmed cargo flow at manifold and duty office in CCR (2Off) confirmed cargo flowing out from 6P and 6S CT as ullages for both tanks were found increasing.  At/around : 1340LT/28June2019  Manifold pressure increased to 4bar on terminal request at 7S Manifold.  At/Around : 1343LT/28June2019  Second officer reported that 7S CT pressure is found rising sharpely and asked duty crew to activate the PV valve manually.  At/around : 1345LT/28June2019  as OS Akash deep rushed to activate the PV valve cargo RBD Palm Oil spilled from 7S CT PV valve due to high pressure in tank.
ImmediateAction: All available crew called. Spill clean up started as per SMPEP, spill contained within the spilled area and spilled cargo collected with scoops/shovels/saw dust. Office notified as per SMPEP.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: SPill of 40-50ltr cargo on deck.
DirectCause: • Direct Cause i. When vessel was discharging RBD palm oil cargo from 6P and 6S using 7S manifold, cargo seeped into 7S COT through cargo pump due to open discharge valve. The valve was not shut after blowing through line from manifold.  ii. Overfill alarm of 7S COT was already in active state due to which OOW was not alerted prior to the cargo spill from PV valve. The High-pressure alarm was activated, however OOW did not corelate activation of high pressure alarm to rise of cargo level to ensure activation of emergency stop from cargo control room.  • Indirect cause i. The OOW failed to check the lineup prior commencement of RBD palm oil discharge to verify status quo of cargo valves prior commencement of cargo operation to confirm the idle tank valve were in closed condition prior starting the cargo. ii. The OOW failed to monitor ullages of Cargo tanks which were idle during the initial stages of discharge operation. iii. De-bottoming of cargo tanks was not considered to bring down the level of cargo tanks to below 90% to facilitate availability of 95 % and 98% alarms to alert OOW in monitoring any rise in the cargo level.  • Root Cause i. Pre-occupation with other task/Distraction: Chief officer being on deck rounds with Sire inspector should have either delayed the commencement of next parcel or requested inspector to defer the deck rounds ii. Inadequate Situational awareness/Risk perception/Risk awareness: • The overfill alarm of 7S COT was in the activate state which did not alert OOW about the rise of cargo into 7S COT. • The lineup was not checked and verified by both OOW and Chief officer to confirm the idle tank valve were in closed condition prior starting the cargo. • Company procedures required that all cargo tanks with the same cargo to be discharged to below 90% before commencement of bulk discharge was not adhered to.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Deck watchkeepers briefing was carried out by Master on verification of valve open/close position and follow up verification by chief officer prior commencement/resumin g cargo operation.
Corrective Action: Deck watchkeepers briefing was carried out by Master on verification of valve open/close position and follow up verification by chief officer prior commencement/resumin g cargo operation.
LessionLearnt: i. After cargo sampling, the lineup should be checked and verified by both OOW and Chief officer so as to confirm the idle tank valve are in closed condition prior starting the cargo ii. During discharging/loading it is important to monitor ullages of other tanks in line up which are not being discharged/loaded for any unexpected changed iii. Any deviation from Cargo plan, one of the Senior officers should be present in the CCR for overview and control the plan instead of relying on the OOW especially when any simultaneous operation is in progress, e.g. Sire / PSC Inspection iv. If any situation develops, such as activation of alarms during discharging or load operation which may eventually lead to spill/leak/damage cargo operation should be immediately stopped to investigate the case and find the fault prior resuming operations. v. High High level (Overfill) alarm if in active state, due to vessel trim during the course of discharging other different parcels, ship staff should ensure that vessel trim should be reduced to open the High High-level alarm condition to alert OOW whenever such situation arises. Prior bulk discharge, all tanks to be reduce below 90% of the tank capacity irrespective of the conditions. vi. Verification process of line at all levels of cargo operations including line up verification when resuming cargo operation.

IncidentDetails: After Heaving up Port anchor at Zhuhai Anchorage area K 21, While tightening of brakes, the regulating screw rod of brake assembly broke,
ImmediateAction: Anchor was lashed with additonal lashing wires and bow stopper secured.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Regulating Screw rod of brake assembly of port side anchor borken
DirectCause: Material fatigue/ failure
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Renewal of Regulating Screw rod
Corrective Action: Renewal of Regulating Screw rod
LessionLearnt: To continue with good inspection and get alert when any defect noticed
ReviewComments: Discussed among ships staff
ClosingNote: Spare rod also kept onboard.

IncidentDetails: After Discharging at Ulsan, it was noticed that all bellows on No.2 Cargo line were deformed.
IncidentEventDetails: After completion of cargo operations, all valves in the lines were closed, which led to liquid trap in the No.2 Cargo line, causing all bellows of the line to expand and get deformed.
ImmediateAction: Immediately inspected all bellows of all other lines. All lines on deck and in pumproom checked for any leaks and damages. No damge or deformation found in other bellows or lines. PR for new bellows raised.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: All three bellows of no.2 cargo line found deformed.
DirectCause: 1) Lack of Skills : Officers and Crew didn't checked and confirmed that no gaseous liquid is trapped in cargo line , prior closing all associated valves.  2) Lack of Supervision : PIC supervising the entire operation, didn't ensured that lines are well stripped and empty prior closing all valves. additionally he forgot to get the valves opened after sailing despite having knowledge of trapped liquid in the cargo line. 3) Mentally Inadequate : PIC for cargo operations was having domestic problems at home, which was distracting him from concentrating properly on his routine chores, this might be one of the contributory factor for lack of supervision.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1. A well legible poster to be posted in CCR near  cargo console, clearly warning the PIC and other officers to conduct a good draining / stripping of Cargo lines post cargo operation and thereby reminding them to ensure that all associated valves on main cargo lines are kept in open condition to avoid any gassing up and thermal expansion. 2.  Ch.Off to include in cargo plans instructions regarding draining of main lines and keeping drop valves/ line masters open upon completion of cargo operation. Same to be discussed during pre-cargo meetings with all officers and crew. 3.  Handing Over notes of Master's and Ch.Off's to include references with respect to sensitive nature of Bellows on main cargo lines and their usual reasons for their expansion/damage.  4. Ch.off Standing orders in CCR amended to add instructions regarding draining of main lines and keeping drop valves/ line masters open upon completion of cargo operation 5. During pre- joining briefing of Master's and Ch.Off's, subject NM should be discussed with special emphasis on the sensitive nature of Bellows on main cargo lines and their usual reasons for their expansion/damage.
Corrective Action: 1. A well legible poster to be posted in CCR near  cargo console, clearly warning the PIC and other officers to conduct a good draining / stripping of Cargo lines post cargo operation and thereby reminding them to ensure that all associated valves on main cargo lines are kept in open condition to avoid any gassing up and thermal expansion. 2.  Ch.Off to include in cargo plans instructions regarding draining of main lines and keeping drop valves/ line masters open upon completion of cargo operation. Same to be discussed during pre-cargo meetings with all officers and crew. 3.  Handing Over notes of Master's and Ch.Off's to include references with respect to sensitive nature of Bellows on main cargo lines and their usual reasons for their expansion/damage.  4. Ch.off Standing orders in CCR amended to add instructions regarding draining of main lines and keeping drop valves/ line masters open upon completion of cargo operation 5. During pre- joining briefing of Master's and Ch.Off's, subject NM should be discussed with special emphasis on the sensitive nature of Bellows on main cargo lines and their usual reasons for their expansion/damage.
ClosingNote: All corrective actions objective evidence verified, Job Closed 22/07

IncidentDetails: When checking the lifeboat equipments in the starboard side lifeboat, it was found that the four rockect parachute signals as well as the six hand flares were missing.
IncidentEventDetails: On 2nd July, Chief Officer instructed newly joined 3rd Officer to check the lifeboat equipments in the afternoon for good order sake. On checking the stbd lifeboat found that the four rockect parachute signals as well as the six hand flares were missing.
ImmediateAction: Immediately the life boat was searched again, also all the LSA/FFA & deck stores were checked. All ship staff were enquired about the missing items and could gather no information regarding same.  The same was reported to the Office.  Vessel had six spare rocket parachute signals, out which four was put back in the lifeboat immediately.  For the hand flares, a PR SPR/SENA/D/19/)039 was raised.
DirectCause: NIL
Proposedcorrectiveactions: NIL
Corrective Action: The missing lifeboat pyrotechnics to be replaced by new.
ClosingNote: Advised crew to take extra precautions while suez transit for LSA/FFA against pilferage.

IncidentDetails: Vessel was transiting Suez canal south bound convoy on 19-04-2019 and own vessel  was no : 28 in the convoy . At 1730 LT pilot informed that Vessel no: 24 ( 4 vessels  ahead of own V/l ) had steering failure and ran aground .Immediately own vessel  reduced speed and planned to moored the vessel in the nearest mooring buoy .Pilot  requested for tug assistance and same did not arrive in time since all vessels in the  convoy were requesting for tugs. Mooring Stations FWD and AFT were called and  mooring lines were kept ready to pass to mooring boat to make fast with buoy. Initial  plan was to drop anchor and to Moore the vessel on port side. At this time wind  speed was around force 4 and current was about 2 kts SE ly( With the vessel) .As  soon as the vessel dropped anchor( STBD Anchor , Appx 1.5 Shackles on deck) the  mooring boat was lowered and vessel started to pass the rope to boat but in due time  the current caught the beam of vessel and started to swing to STBD , same causing  difficulty to pass the mooring line to the mooring buoy . The swing of the vessel  started to increase and also restricted to usage of main engine due to risk of  propeller getting fouled by the slack mooring rope in the water. At this point master  decide to abort the mooring plan and decide to take a U turn with the help of anchor ,  pilot agreed with the plan .Vessel took the turn successfully with help of STBD  anchor underfoot , ME, wind and current .While staying in the channel and  maintaining safe UKC vessel started to steaming in the reciprocal course North  bound towards Great Biter Lake for E 4 Anchorage as instructed to the pilot by Suez  port control .Vessel dropped Anchor safely at 1954 Hrs and the emergency stations  called off.

IncidentDetails: During heavy weather AM WHIP ANTENNA has been broken on vertical body.  Antenna maker: HANSHIN  Type:  HWS-402LD  Q'ty : 1
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel was expierencing very strong winds around 55-60kts.for last 3 days.One of the AM Antenna on Monkey Island had broken down and fell on the bridge wing.
ImmediateAction: Broken parts of antenna stored in accommodation.Reqsn will be raised for spare antenna and same will be replaced once supplied onboard.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Antenna for AM radio damaged.
DirectCause: Vessel was expierencing very strong winds around 55-60kts.for last 3 days.One of the AM Antenna on Monkey Island had broken down and fell on the bridge wing.Heavy weather and excessive rolling can be attributed towards the cause of Antenna breakdown.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Broken parts of antenna stored in accommodation.Reqsn raised for spare antenna and same will be replaced once supplied onboard.
Corrective Action: Spare part for the AM whip antennae to be connected to vessel at the first available opportunity & to be fitted in place
ClosingNote: Broken antennae replaced

IncidentDetails: At  around 2345 hrs lt  on 10.07.2019 vessel was experiencing strong swell from her stern due to which she was experiencing  rolling  and swaying at Berth NO.6 , loading master was immediately informed.  The vessel crew were stand by to check any movement of the vessel. Due to excessive rolling at berth  as it's a sea berth, Vessel in conjunction with terminal decided to stop discharging and disconnect arms and then to align the vessel keeping safety in mind.  At 0036 hrs on 11.07.2019  stopped unloading as it was observed that the vessel was surging forward and swaying due to strong  swell on her stern. Vessel had surged by about 1.0 mtrs forward from  her actual alignment.  For conducting a safe operation the unloading arm drained & disconnected before Wharfing .Vessel was brought back to position & than loading arms were re-connected. Vessel resumed unloading operations safely.
IncidentEventDetails: 10July2019  2345 LT : Vessel experienced strong swell from her stern due to which she was experiencing  rolling  and swaying. Loading Master informed.All crew were standby in fwd & aft stations.  11July 2019  0036 LT : Unloading stopped / Vl was found shifted by 1.0mtr fwd(approx)  0036 LT : Commneced draining of unloading arm.  0106 LT : Commmenced disconnection of Unloading arm.  0130 LT : Completed disconnection of unloading arm.  0130 - 0200 LT : V/l wharfed back in position.   0200 - 0306 LT : V/l awaiting shore readiness to connect unloading arm.  0306 - 0318 LT : Unloading arm connected  0318 - 0336 LT: V/l awaiting shore readiness to resume discahrging operations.  0336 LT : V/l resumed discharging operations.  1030 LT : V/l safely completed discharge operations.
ImmediateAction: 1) Loading master informed   2) Crew standby fwd & aft stations.  3) Stopped discharging operations.  4) Unloading arm drained & disconnected.  5) Vl wharfed back in position.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Delay in Unloading operations
DirectCause: Open to  sea & unsuitable berth for this size of the vessel
Proposedcorrectiveactions: As a practise while tending mooring fwd & aft moorings to be adjusted at the same time( With sufficient crew)
Corrective Action: Carry out a debriefing session on the incident
ClosingNote: Appreciate masters decision to carry out the shifting of vessel safely after disconnecting the loading arm.

IncidentDetails: While taking round on deck , white paint splash was noticed  on poop deck port quarter and ship’s over side too.  Investigation was carried out with ship staff. But it could not be determined how white paint splashed over there.
IncidentEventDetails: 19/07/2019 1700 LT : Round was taken on deck and everything was in order   20/07/2019 1100 LT :While taking round on deck white paint splash was noticed on poop deck port quarter and ship’s over side too.  20/07/2019 1200 LT : Completed the investigation with ship staff  20/07/2019 1230 LT : Office was reported
ImmediateAction: Entry was made in Garbagel log book . Office was informed . Meeting was carried out with ships staff and matter was discussed
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: PAINT FELL INTO WATER CAUSING POLLUTION
DirectCause: Unattended paint drum carried away by wind and dropped to sea
Proposedcorrectiveactions: OFFICE WAS INFORMED. ALL CREW WERE MUSTERED AND BRIEFING WAS CARRIED OUT . ENTRY WAS MADE IN GARBAGE RECORD BOOK
Cause Analysis: Failure to follow rules and regulations
ClosingNote: A detailed briefing was carried out by Master and Chief Officer to all ship staff and it was stressed to comply with the requirement of not leaving any loose items unattended - Crew briefing was carried out by Marine Supdt during the visit to Hound point on the 21st July'2019

IncidentDetails: While adjusting fwd spring mooring wire, during the discharging at beira - One of the Spring wire rope parted.
IncidentEventDetails: 1610 : Duty AB & OS were adjusing moorings  1612 :"While trying to adjust fwd springs one by one, the weight came on a single spring rope due to  which the wire rope parted (At the end of eye were the mandal shackle is connected).  1613 : Duty AB reported immediately to CCR  1613 : Duty Officer inturn alerted Master & CO  1614 : Crew Were called for Stations, parted wire removed from the Shore bollard.  1620 : Second Spring + 1 Additional spring rope passed on to the shore bollard as a replacement for the parted Wire.  1620 - 1625 : All Deck crew were briefed / Trained on the root cause of the incident and alerted them  to keep a close watch on the Moorings.
ImmediateAction: Damaged wire rope removed from the Shore bollard & replaced with the New spring rope + 1 Additional Spring rope (Soft Mooring rope)
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: One wire rope parted
DirectCause: poor condioned wire and improper handling of wires
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Requesition raised for hawsers and vsl planned to replace all 4 nos of wire rope to soft rope.
Corrective Action: Forward and aft spring wire ropes to be changed to soft ropes.
ClosingNote: Ropes renewed.

IncidentDetails: Vessel while discharging at Tauranga, New Zealand experienced Boiler burner trouble. Boiler was being used on the vessel for IG system. IG supply was disrupted due to this Boiler trouble. Troubleshooting was done and it was noted that Burner Rotary Cup bearing failed. Bearing was renewed and boiler put back in use. Vessel had a cargo stoppage of approximately 5 hours due to this trouble. Though Vessel reported to the Charterer Commercial team, it was noted that the vessel failed to file an Emergency Incident Report as required by the Charterer’s Voyage Orders.
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel commenced cargo discharging operation at Tauranga terminal, New Zealand, on 10th June 2019/ 1254LT. AT 1448hrs Lt. essel observed vibration in rotary burner of the boiler, and the boiler was stopped immediately. Since boiler was being used for IG operation, cargo operation had to be stopped. Boiler burner was opened after taking all precautions. On opening, it was observed that the rotary shaft bearing was damaged. Burner was dismantled and bearing renewed. Vessel reported to Charterers Operations via email at 1518 hours. After completion of repairs, the rotary burner belt alignment and tightness was checked. Boiler operation checked to be working satisfactory. Boiler was put in use and IG plant restarted. Cargo operation resumed on 10th June 2019/ 1940 hrs LT which resulted in a stoppage of 4hrs 52 min.  Vessel reported the resumption of discharge operations to the Charterers operations team at 2002 hours. However, vessel reported the incident to the charterers Emergency Notification center on 11 June at 1043 hours.  End of Sea passage (ESP)      : 10th June 19 / 0630 LT   Arrival Date /Time at Discharging Port    : 10th June 19 / 0630 LT   Time loading hose(s) connected     : 10th June 19 / 1037 LT   Time vessel commenced Discharge     : 10th June 19 / 1254 LT    Time Discharge suspended due to Malfunction of Ship's Boiler : 10th June 19 / 1448 LT   Vessel reported to the Charterers Operations team   : 10th June 19 / 1518 LT    Time Vessel completed Boiler Repairs and Resumed discharge : 10th June 19 / 1940 LT   Vessel reported resumption of cargo to charterers operations : 10th June 19 / 2002 LT   Vessel made Emergency Notification to Charterers Emergency : 11th June 19 / 1043 LT
ImmediateAction: Boiler rotary burner bearing found damaged same replaced with new spare bearing which was on board. The system was started and was used without any further interruption and the discharging operation was completed smoothly.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1) Delay due to suspension of discharging. 2) Customer dissatisfaction as the reporting was not made as per the Voyage Orders.
DirectCause: Probable Cause for the failure rotary burner bearing:    a) Bearing Failure:    Most probable cause for the failure of the bearings is the misalignment and inappropriate tension of the drive belt.     The running burner was closely monitored until completion of the discharge. The alignment and tension on the belt were rechecked after the discharging and found to be in order.  Root Cause:    The PMS did not include the following guidelines by the makers and hence had missed out by the ship staff when performing the inspection and maintenance using the guidelines given in the PMS:    1. The burner main shaft ball bearings should be lubricated with 4~5 grams of grease every 1000 running hours.  2. The Burner V belt tension should be adjusted after the first 50~100 hours burner operating period after overhaul.     b) Delayed reporting of the Boiler breakdown to the charterers Emergency Notification center:      The delay in reporting to the charterers Emergency Notification center can be attributed to the misinterpretation of the Voyage Order requirements by the vessel.     Root Cause:    Master had considered that this incident has not caused any damage or a dangerous situation to the vessel and hence need to be reported only to Charterer’s commercial team and that there was no need for an Emergency Notification.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: ACTIONS TAKEN TO PREVENT SIMILAR OCCURRENCE:     • This incident of Boiler breakdown will be circulated across the fleet, highlighting the importance of capturing all aspects of the maintenance in the PMS (even if it is mentioned separately as Notes).   PIC: Technical Superintendent Target action Date: Draft Completed. Will be Circulated on 28th June 2019.    • This incident of delayed reporting to the Emergency Notification was circulated across the fleet, stressing on the need to carefully assess the situation in order to ensure that the communication is prompt as required by the Voyage Orders.   PIC: Marine Superintendent Target action Date: Completed.    • The engineer in charge for boiler maintenance was briefed and trained by Chief Engineer on proper checking of belt alignment and tightness.   PIC: Technical Superintendent Target action Date: Completed.    • All engineers were briefed to carry out pre arrival check at least 2 days in advance so as to conduct the belt tightness check before discharge operation.  PIC: Technical Superintendent Target action date: Completed.    • Following to be included in the PMS job description for Auxiliary Boiler: 1. The burner main shaft ball bearings should be lubricated with 4~5 grams of grease every 1000 running hours. 2. The Burner V belt tension should be adjusted after the first 50~100 hours burner operating period after overhaul.   PIC: Technical Superintendent                               Target action date: Completed.    •  Master was reminded by the Marine Superintendent who had attended the vessel for overseeing the SIRE inspection, to carefully assess the Voyage Order requirements with regards to Charterer’s Emergency Notification requirements. The importance of re-evaluating stoppages and consequent Off-hire loss which may result in actual Loss of Hire to be significantly Higher than the original estimates should be closely borne in mind.  PIC: Marine Superintendent                                  Target action date: Continuous.
Corrective Action: ACTIONS TAKEN TO PREVENT SIMILAR OCCURRENCE:    This incident of Boiler breakdown will be circulated across the fleet, highlighting the importance of capturing all aspects of the maintenance in the PMS (even if it is mentioned separately as Notes). IncidentDetails: Vessel sailing from Singapore anchorage to Rotterdam on 10th March 2019, found that HFO & LSMGO Sample bottles of Zoushan ship yard (3 HFO & 3 LSMGO) and LSMGO Singapore bunker (3 LSMGO) TOTAL 9 Sample bottles missing from paint locker at sea.
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel bunkered HSFO: 2172 MT AND LSMGO: 135 MT from ZOUSHAN ship yard on 08.01.2019, samples have taken through continuous drip sampler from ships manifold and transferred  to four bunker sample containers which are as follows  : i) one set for vessel retention, ii) one set for vessel MARPOL, iii) one set for barge & iv) one set for lab analysis.   After bunkering completed one set of bottles has been sent for lab analysis and one set given to barge, other 3 sets of samples including samples from barge /supplier (1 set) received on board (i.e. 3 HFO and 3 LSMGO) which were kept in paint locker by ship staff. Since vessel just departed from yard there was no permanent box available for storing all samples including MARPOL samples and crew kept those samples in a bucket and secured them in paint locker.   Then vessel reached SINGAPORE anchorage on 07th February 2019 for LSMGO bunker. Vessel took bunker of 76.008 MT LSMGO., Samples have taken through continuous drip sampler from ships manifold and transfer to four bunker containers which is as follows:  i) one for vessel retention, ii) one for vessel MARPOL, iii) one for barge & iv) one for lab analysis. after bunkering completed one bottles has sent for lab analysis and one given to barge, other 3 samples including samples from barge manifold remained on board.  While keeping the 3 LSMGO Sample’s  of Singapore bunker on departure Singapore anchorage on 07.02.2019. by ship’s crew in paint locker, he confirmed that the other 6 sample bottles from ZOUSHAN ship yard were also available in paint locker in a bucket ( as those were kept earlier) . All 9 sample bottles were kept in a bucket in paint locker on departure Singapore.  Later, MARPOL sample box has been fabricated by crew on a long voyage from Singapore to Rotterdam ON, 10th March 2019 after fabrication of the Sample box, ship staff went to paint locker to transfer sample bottles to the MARPOL box & found that the bunker samples (all 9 bottles) were missing along with the bucket in paint locker. Same was informed to Chief engineer and Master & later reported to company.
ImmediateAction: 1) Informed Office,  2) Flag State was informed by office & Official Log Entry was made for the incident as advised by Flag State.  3) Searching for the Missing Sample Bottles continues,  4) New samples with New seals taken for all Respective Fuel & Diesel Oils & stored in MARPOL Box with Lock & key in Paint Locker.  5) Key is kept under direct custody of chief engineer
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Vessel bunkered HSFO: 2172 MT AND LSMGO: 135 MT from ZOUSHAN ship yard on 08.01.2019, samples have taken through continuous drip sampler from ships manifold and transferred  to four bunker sample containers which are as follows  : i) one set for vessel retention, ii) one set for vessel MARPOL, iii) one set for barge & iv) one set for lab analysis.   After bunkering completed one set of bottles has been sent for lab analysis and one set given to barge, other 3 sets of samples including samples from barge /supplier (1 set) received on board (i.e. 3 HFO and 3 LSMGO) which were kept in paint locker by ship staff. Since vessel just departed from yard there was no permanent box available for storing all samples including MARPOL samples and crew kept those samples in a bucket and secured them in paint locker.   Then vessel reached SINGAPORE anchorage on 07th February 2019 for LSMGO bunker. Vessel took bunker of 76.008 MT LSMGO., Samples have taken through continuous drip sampler from ships manifold and transfer to four bunker containers which is as follows:  i) one for vessel retention, ii) one for vessel MARPOL, iii) one for barge & iv) one for lab analysis. after bunkering completed one bottles has sent for lab analysis and one given to barge, other 3 samples including samples from barge manifold remained on board.  While keeping the 3 LSMGO Sample’s  of Singapore bunker on departure Singapore anchorage on 07.02.2019. by ship’s crew in paint locker, he confirmed that the other 6 sample bottles from ZOUSHAN ship yard were also available in paint locker in a bucket ( as those were kept earlier) . All 9 sample bottles were kept in a bucket in paint locker on departure Singapore.  Later, MARPOL sample box has been fabricated by crew on a long voyage from Singapore to Rotterdam ON, 10th March 2019 after fabrication of the Sample box, ship staff went to paint locker to transfer sample bottles to the MARPOL box & found that the bunker samples (all 9 bottles) were missing along with the bucket in paint locker. Same was informed to Chief engineer and Master & later reported to company.
DirectCause: Marpol sample bottles not kept under lock and seal arrangement and not marked distinguished in Paint locker.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Seperate box to be made for storage of Marpol sample bottles and kept under lock and seal arrangement to be made and kept to be kept under direct custody of Chief engineer.
Corrective Action: Seperate box to be made for storage of Marpol sample bottles and kept under lock and seal arrangement to be made and kept to be kept under direct custody of Chief engineer.
Cause Analysis: Failure to follow rules and regulations
LessionLearnt: MARPOL SAMPLES to be kept secured and locked.
ReviewComments: Ships staff told to be cautious and alert with regard to samples.
ClosingNote: Discussed with ship staff

IncidentDetails: During transit from St.Rose to Geismar found no 2 and 5 unit Exhaust valve close and open position changed alarm, and engine slow down activated, rpm not increasing beyond Slow Ahd. Vsl anchored at reserve anchorage, Class surveyor inspected and issued report but Amended COTP Order “COTP Order 0405-19 Amendment 1".Escort tug assistance requested from reserve anchorage  to Geismar. After departure from Geismar vsl anchored at White Castle anchorage for MAN service engineer inspection. After inspection and  trouble shooting, technician issued report, the trouble was due to outdated software, the software was updated by the attending technician, the report was then sent to USCG and restriction removed.
IncidentEventDetails: During transit from St.Rose to Geismar, engine stopped and again moment given. During this time the alarm  Exhaust valve close and open position changed for no 2 and 5 unit sounded.Due to this alarm engine slow down activated and rpmfound  not increasing .
ImmediateAction: Alarm was resetted, meantime tugs assistance requested, vsl was shifted to reserve anchorage, class surveyor inspection carried out.
DirectCause: Overall the outdated software which is installed in ECS, which is not able to supervise the exhaust valve operation when the crankshaft is wind milling is the cause of the problem happened. With old version software provisions are not provided for monitoring the exhaust valve function when the crankshaft is wind milling, which gave alarm “Exhaust valve open/close position changed”. Due to the alarm engine slow down activated and couldn’t increase rpm during the river transit and the vessel was restricted by USCG with escort tugs during the passage from reserve anchorage to Geismar and to White castle anchorage involving additional pilotage and tugs assistance.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: MAN service engineer inspected and trouble shooting carried out. MAN B&W Service technician boarded the vessel and started carrying out his checks. Exhaust valve drop down test was carried out & result was satisfactory. MAN B&W Service technician carried out the function tests for ELFI & FIVA valves & was satisfied with the performance. Service technician however suspected of “wind milling” of crankshaft which resulted in the alarm & ME Slowdown.   Wind milling occurs when ordered RPM is 0, fuel index is 0 but crankshaft is turning. This can happen when the crankshaft has not yet stopped turning from the previous bell. MAN B&W Service technician stated that the current software loaded does not identify this wind milling effect and therefore, the alarm caused. After consulting with MAN B&W Head-office in Copen-Hagen, the service engineer updated the software on MOP A, MOP B & PMI ECS computer. Engines were again tried out after upgradation of software’s, & found all in good working order.
ClosingNote: USCG hold was cleared after the technician sent the report.

IncidentDetails: During our loading operation at Lubuk Gaung, SDS terminal St 1 , Indonesia on 28 July 2019 at 0255 lt  4 armed robbers were found near engine room work shop area, alarm was raised. On noticing the engine crew and hearing the alarm the robber fled away from steering gear room , lock of steering gear room door was found broken/ opened. Agent /terminal / PFSO through agent was informed,spare from Engine room stolen.  Vessel / Terminal was maintaining Marsec level I.
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel was berthed port side alongside at SDS St 1 berth.  Marsec level at port : Level 1    All external doors of accomodation , stores were locked from outside except A deck stbd side door while Emergency Escape door from E/R , Emergency generator room , CO2 room  doors were sealed.    Three watch keepers were present during the watch , 1 at the gangway , 1 taking safety security rounds and attending to ropes as required  and 1 person assisting for loading / sampling .    0255 Lt : Oiler Satheesh informed duty officer robber sighted inside Engine room  0255 Lt : Announcement was made by duty officer about robbers in Engine room  0256 Lt : Terminal informed through cargo surveyor  0257 Lt : General emergency alarm raised  0300 Lt : Crew mustered and head count taken, commence search  0305 Lt : Agent informed and asked to inform PFSO  0318 Lt : CSO informed  0326 Lt : Shore security person onboard  0400 Lt : Search completed , no robbers found onboard, Emergency Escape door from E/R , Emergency generator room , CO2 room door seal found intact and lock of strg gear room found opened by force.
ImmediateAction: Alarm raised , informed agent, terminal, PFSO through agent , CSO
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 4 discarded and 1 new generator's plunger barrel , 1 vernier calliper stolen
DirectCause: Breach of security
Proposedcorrectiveactions: To employ shore watchman
Corrective Action: 1.A security circular to be shared with all the vessels briefing the incident and reminding the ship-staff to plan the watch compositions always considering the prevailing operations and conditions in port. Additional personnel from E/R should be deployed on deck (if required considering security guards) to ensure all parts of deck are patrolled at all times.
ClosingNote: closed

IncidentDetails: Vessel was carrying out lowering of both lifeboats in water at Gothenburg ‘A’ anchorage on 30-Jul-2019, after checking with Local agent and taking prior permission from VTS.    Port life boat was initially lowered with painter tied to fwd and aft before lowering.    While the lifeboat sprinkler was being tried out, the boat drifted towards the vessel’s hull and went underneath the curvature below ship’s accommodation.    The stbd. side porthole of the lifeboat frame got pressed against the hull and the toughened glass got cracked from one edge, before the boat crew could manage to push the boat away from ship’s hull using boat hooks.
IncidentEventDetails: Vessel was carrying out lowering of both lifeboats in water at Gothenburg ‘A’ anchorage on 30-Jul-2019, after checking with Local agent and taking prior permission from VTS.    Port life boat was initially lowered with painter tied to fwd and aft before lowering.    While the lifeboat sprinkler was being tried out, the boat drifted towards the vessel’s hull and went underneath the curvature below ship’s accommodation.    The stbd. side porthole of the lifeboat frame got pressed against the hull and the toughened glass got cracked from one edge, before the boat crew could manage to push the boat away from ship’s hull using boat hooks.
ImmediateAction: The boat crew pushed the boat away from ship’s hull using boat hooks. Once all test completed, the boat was safely lifted back on deck and properly secured. Requisition raised for new glass for the L/Boat porthole.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Portside Lifeboats's Stbd side porthole glass got cracked and needs to be renewed. No Personal injury.
DirectCause: Ship staff failed to anticipate the extent of load on the Lifeboat body due to the drift which was pushing the boat against the hull.  Also, preventive measures were not taken in time to keep the boat clear of ships hull.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Lessons learnt from the incident will be discussed with ship staff in  safety Meeting.
Corrective Action: Lessons learnt to be discussed with all ship staff
ClosingNote: Weather conditions to be closely monitored prior/during the boat drills / work aloft.

IncidentDetails: Bay 20 Underdeck reefers loaded with reefer motor facing in the wrong direction.
IncidentEventDetails: 28/0001LT: Cargo operations in progress with 3 gantries .Gantry 1 on Bay 20 ,Gantry 2 on Bay36 (loading  Reefers) and Gantry 3 on Bay 48.  28/0015LT: Commenced loading of containers (Reefer) in Bay 20 Cargo hold.  28/0245LT: Completed loading on Bay 20 Cargo hold.  28/0015-0245LT: Ship crew were unplugging reefers on Bay32 (Foreman instructed after completion 0f Bay 36 gantry will shift to Bay 32) and plugging reefers on Bay36.  28/0255LT: Ship crew checked Reefers were wrongly loaded (Motors facing in opposite direction and cannot plug in) in Bay 20 Cargo hold and immediately informed foreman.  28/0300LT: Bay 20 Hatch cover Closed. Vessel was in communication with Foreman and duty officer informed chief officer.  28/0450LT: Informed agent about Reefers loaded wrongly in Bay20.  28/0400LT-0900LT: Vessel was in contact with agent, planner, terminal & charterers.  28/0935LT: Gantry started re-stowing of containers in Bay20.  28/0015LT-1100LT: Vessel unable to connect reefer due to wrong stowage.
ImmediateAction: Informed foreman, agent, planner & charterers and arranged for restow of bay 20 Underdeck reefers.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Additional costs involved in restow of 18 X Reefers and 30 X General containers in Bay 20 underdeck.
DirectCause: Duty foreman failed to confirm reefer motor direction at the time before loading.Also duty watchkeepers were busy in plugging and unplugging reefers on other bays and failed to notice reefer being loaded with motor facing wrong direction.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1) Before loading operations, Reefer motor facing side for each bay to be acknowledged and signed by Foreman/Planner or Cargo Supervisor.  2) Duty personnel to be more vigilant and check each bay as soon as loading is commenced.
ClosingNote: Regular monitoring of reefer units while loading is critical.

IncidentDetails: After completing the Ship Shore Safety Meeting at 1530 LT today, Loading Master requested for the "Stadis 450" Drums (6 in no.) to be lowered on to the jetty.   By approx. 1545 LT, vessel had already lowered Five (5) drums on to the jetty. While lowering the last drum, the rope gave away and the drum got accidentally released into the sea.  The drum was, however, intact and sealed and there was no spillage from the drum, whatsoever.  Ship's Crew together with Loading Master tried to pick up the drum from the sea but due to strong currents in the river, same could not be retrieved and went beyond reach. Loading Master also could not arrange any Mooring Boat for this purpose.  The drum was afloat and partly visible above the water until it was in sight.
IncidentEventDetails: 1. 1530 - Completed Shipshore Safety Meeting  2. 1535 - Commenced Lowering "Stadis 450" Drums to jetty using Heaving line./ Manifold Connection in Progress.  3. 1545 - While lowering Last drum of "STADIS 450 " Heaving line gave away and drum fell into the sea.
ImmediateAction: 1. Initial Notification made to SPRO   2. Notification made to Agent / Office / Owners / Charterers / All parties as per Voyage Orders.  3. SPRO mobilised their response Barge "HU NAN YOU 10" to look for the drum all the way from Pudong Airport till the vessel's current location but unfortunately same could not be sighted.  4. SPRO also confirmed that there were NO visible traces of any chemical or similar in the water, sighted by the Barge, while carrying out their search for the drum.  5. Entry made in Garbage Record Book for Accidental loss of "STADIS 450" Drum.  6. Agents confirmed that Vessel does not need to report to MSA. Same shall be done at their end, if required.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1. Loss of 1 No."STADIS 450" Drum into the sea. No Pollution Reported.  2.SPRO confirmed that there were NO visible traces of any chemical or similar in the water, sighted by the Barge, while carrying out their search for the drum.
DirectCause: Lack of Planning, Inadequate Supervision
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Such enviornmentally sensitive chemicals contained in Drums (20 ltrs. or so) to be preferably hand lowered on to the jetty and ropes, if to be used, to be thoroughly checked by a responsible person prior being put into use for wear and tear and such operation to be carried out with Deck Officer in attendance and with close liasion with Surveyor/Loading MAster, as the case may be.
Corrective Action: Such enviornmentally sensitive chemicals contained in Drums (20 ltrs. or so) to be preferably hand lowered on to the jetty and ropes, if to be used, to be thoroughly checked by a responsible person prior being put into use for wear and tear and such operation to be carried out with Deck Officer in attendance and with close liasion with Surveyor/Loading MAster, as the case may be.
ClosingNote: Job Closed 24/07

IncidentDetails: While maneuvering to Pick up Pilot at Houston Pilot station, the steering gear number 1 gave overload alarm and the steering motor tripped. Upon investigation it was found that the steering pump ceased.
IncidentEventDetails: 1031 : overload alarm activated and Steering motor number 1 tripped . No 2 steering gear was in operation and working satisfactoriily. Vessel was on hand steering and was approaching to pilot station  1032 : started steering motor number 1 and it tripped immediately.   1035 : Insulation tested for the motor, found satisfactory. Engines stopped to adjust ETA to Pilot station  1040 : checked coupling, found that pump not rotating by hand  1043 : Pilot station informed regarding the malfunction of steering gear No.1. Vessel was advised that Pilot will board the vessel and assess the situation.  1046 : started disconnecting motor from the sterering  pump  1056 : Motor disconnected from the steering pump No. 1 and found motor found Normal , pump found ceased  1118 : Pilot on board.  1120 : Risk assessment conducted along with the Pilot. Pilot informed Coast guard and vessel was advised to take Escort tug. Vessel proceeding through the channel with operational #2 steering pump.  1250 : No. 1 steering pump replaced with overhauled pump, oil taken and pump started. Forund working satisfactorily. Pilot informed coast guard regarding the same.
ImmediateAction: Re-started No.1 steering gear motor , the same tripped again giving overload alarm. No. 1 steering pump started locatly, the same tripped again. Checked functioning of no. 2 steering gear found working satisfactory.Informed Company , Houston Pilot station and Agent  regarding the malfunction of steeing gear No. 1. Vessel instructed to proceed to Pick up Pilot by Pilot station  Deficiency noted  on Pilot/Master information exchange. pilot boarded the vessel and Risk assessment carried out along with the Pilot .  Vessel had crossed abort position and joint decision to continue on the passage with Escort tug was taken. Repair work for motor was started.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Steering Pump ceased and was in-operational
DirectCause: Due to ageing and material failure the pump rotor ceased
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Steering pump renewed with overhauled pump. The in-operational pump to be serviced and made operational and kept as spare pump
Corrective Action: Steering gear LO to be sent for analysis
ClosingNote: Steering gear LO shore testing is done and found in good condition

IncidentDetails: During Vessel's call at Ulsan for Loading Mogas, after completing the Tank Inspection, Surveyor confirmed with the vessel reg the Tank Cleaning carried out. Vessel informed the Surveyor that for Mogas (basis last cargo as Jet A-1) no cleaning is required. Surveyor then informed the vessel that Tank Washing  is required to load Mogas as the Sulphur content of the Mogas is less than 10 ppm and the vessel last carried cargo (Jet A-1) had high content of sulphur which will cause the Cargo to be contaminated. Vessel informed the surveyor that Vessel was not informed anytime that the cargo to be loaded is Mogas 10 PPM and the Voyage orders clearly state cargo name as "MOGAS" only. After further discussion with all the relevant parties (Office, Owners, Charterers), it was decided that the vessel has to Unberth, Carry out complete Tank washing with Sea Water and then Rinse the tank with Fresh water and then drain all the lines and tanks completely.
IncidentEventDetails: 1. Vessel Arrived Load port- Ulsan, S.Korea on 04th July 2019 / 0554 LT  2. Vessel Tender NOR - 06th July 2019 / 0001 LT  3. Vessel All Fast at Terminal SK No.7 - 08th July 2019 / 1954 LT  4. Vessel Tank Inspection -  08th July 2019 / 2100-2310  LT.  5. Surveyor Informed Vessel all Tanks are dry.  6. Surveyor after consultation with the Charterers inform vessel Inspection failed and vessel has to unberth and carry out Tank cleaning prior Loading.  7. Vessel Unberthed from Terminal S K No.7  - 09th July 2019 / 0324 LT.  8. Completed Tank Cleaning and Retendered NOR - 09th July 2019 / 1530 LT
ImmediateAction: 1. Vessel notified all the relevant Parties (Owners, Charterers, Agent, Office).  2. Unberthed from the Jetty and proceeded out of port Limit to carry out Tank cleaning.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Vessel was asked to Unberth and carry out Tank cleaning out of Port Limit and after Completing Tank cleaning vessel to Retender NOR.  Vessel Tank Inspection failed - 2310 LT on 08th July 2019  Vessel Unberthed at  0324 LT on 09th July 2019  Vessel Completed Tank Cleaning and Retendered NOR - 1530 LT on 09th July 2019.
DirectCause: Failure to Warn : Incomplete information in voyage order/voyage instructions/Terminal conditions regarding commodity to be loaded and local requirements for tank preparation.  Lack of Compliance : Voyage order instructions for advising tank cleaning method was not complied with assuming it's a generic statement.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: 1. Master and Ch.Off to carefully check the certificate of quality of last cargo carried onboard and be aware of sulphur content for the same. This information should be shared with the commercial operator while tank preparations for next cargo is discussed. 2. During initial stages of voyage fixtures, if there are two kind of tank cleaning suggested in tank cleaning guides, Kindly advice the most stringent of the two tank preparation method to chrts/ commercial operator/ broker. If required commercial operator may request vessel to use the other tank preparation method thus clearing Master’s stand on the same. 3. Master and Ch.Off  to carefully read and respond to each and every point of the voyage order, even if it appears generic in nature or if it has been answered earlier during voyage fixture.  4. Master to  include information regarding last three cargoes and tank preparation carried out in the remarks sections of Stowage plans send to all parties after receipt of voyage orders. 5. After receipt of voyage order, pls re-confirm with commercial operator if any further cargo specific requirement is expected by receiver including tank cleaning.

IncidentDetails: While heaving up the port side accomodation ladder, wire of the accomodation ladder parted, causing the ladder to fall free by gravity and slam into the sea. Ship railing was damaged due to the rope tied on the gangway which was tied to the railing.
IncidentEventDetails: 1615 hrs: Crew had completed securing the stbd side accomodation ladder.  1620 hrs: Crew began to heave up the port side accomodation ladder to secure it.   1625 hrs: The accomodation ladder came up to the deck level without any problem. At this time the accomodation ladder wire parted just near the winching drum.  1625: Bosun reported to the bridge that the accomodation ladder wire had parted .   1627: Master and chief officer arrived the location. the ladder was partly in the sea at one end, with the turntable holding the gangway at the other end.  1632 : All crew assembled for recovery of the gangway. A short tool box meeting was carried out making a specific plan for the recovery of the gangway.  1640: Tool box meeting completed .Concerned personnel were asked to donn the life vest and safety harness. other crew members began assisting in bringing the required equipments.  1645: Recovery of the gangway comenced using a messager rope with a mandal shackle and using the main deck (aft port side) winch.  1715: The gangway was carefully winched up till the limitthat it could be brought.   1735: Chain blocks were rigged and the recovery of the gangway continued. Master and chief officer along with the bosun considered other plans or possibilities to get the job done without any further damage and injury.  1945: Accomodation ladder was finally brought up completly and stowed in position without any further damages, using chain blocks, messenger and also using the gangway winch. All crew involved were safe and no injuries during the job.
ImmediateAction: Tool box meeting carried out and actions to recover the gangway without further damage was discussed. Superintendent informed.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1. Accomodation ladder turntable platform pin slightly bend.   2. 4 staunctions bend and damaged. 2 lost overboard.  3. 5 Staunction brackets damaged.  4. ship side railing bend.
DirectCause: Old age of wire. Appeared to be in satisfactory condition, but was weak. Was due for renewal in a month.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Thourough inspection of wires to be carried out. over greasing to be avoided which hides the surface.
LessionLearnt: Lack of inspection prior putting in use.  Lack of Monitoring.
ReviewComments: MSC.1/Circ.1331  4 MAINTENANCE  4.1 Accommodation ladders and gangways, including associate winch and fittings, should be properly maintained and inspected at appropriate intervals as required by SOLAS regulation III/20.7.2, in accordance with manufacturers’ instructions. Additional checks should be made each time the accommodation ladder and gangway is rigged, looking out for signs of distortion, cracks and corrosion. Close examination for possible corrosion should be carried out, especially when an aluminium accommodation ladder/gangway has fittings made of mild steel. 4.2 Bent stanchions should be replaced or repaired and guard ropes should be inspected for wear and renewed where necessary. 4.3 Moving parts should be free to turn and should be greased as appropriate. 4.4 The lifting equipment should be inspected, tested and maintained paying careful attention to the condition of the hoist wire. The wires used to support the means of embarkation and disembarkation should be renewed when necessary, as required by SOLAS regulation II-1/3-9. 4.5 Arrangements should also be made to examine the underside of gangways and accommodation ladders at regular intervals. 4.6 All inspections, maintenance work and repairs of accommodation ladders and gangways should be recorded in order to provide an accurate history for each appliance. The information to be recorded appropriately on board should include the date of the most recent inspection, the name of the person or body who carried out that inspection, the due date for the next inspection and the dates of renewal of wires used to support the embarkation and disembarkation arrangement.
ClosingNote: Lack of sufficient check on taking over the vessel , Please review the comments given.

IncidentDetails: During Loading Operations at beaumont it was observed that 1P WBT suction/ filling valve was not holding. tank level was not maintaining. During loaded voyage the valve was inspected, valve was found damaged, the disc spindle is bent and it came out of the body of the valve.
IncidentEventDetails: Tank Level was not holding during operations.  Valve operation was tried out from VRCS and Locally through emergency pump.  It was suspected valve is not holding.
ImmediateAction: The Valve is replaced with an assembled valve (components of old valve from engine roon with a new seat ring).
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: 1P WBT Suction/ filling valve was found damaged, the disc spindle is bent and it came out of the body of the valve.
DirectCause: Material Failure.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: All involved in operation briefed about normal practice of  tank valves to be opened first before opening the seachest line, to avoid pressure surge in the line. maximum filling rate for ballast tanks poster displayed all involved informed about the minimum number of tanks to be opened at any time (use of 1 pump or both pumps together).
ClosingNote: Noted that the valve has been repaired and made operational. New valve is on order and will be connected on board at upcoming port to keep as as spare.

IncidentDetails: During cargo loading operation, there was a sudden heavy noise heard from the P/R area and simultaneously it was observed that No.2 P/R Fan which was in use has stopped.
IncidentEventDetails: 1 - No.2 P/R Fan running  2 - The D-shackle pin which might have got loosened due to  vibration has come out resulting in opening of the D-shackle suddenly  3 - The damper by its own weight has fallen down to 'Down' position as indicated in the earlier sent picture. Same time activating the limit switch which in turn tripped the No.2 P/R Fan.  4 - Sudden closure of damper while Fan in operation created a very heavy back-pressure at the Fan impeller and all the blades gave away at the roots (weakest position) and also there is crack in the centre of the impeller at both the sides (suction side and discharge side).  5 - The pressure surge created a spring action of the damper for a while and the damper partly opened and closed couple of times. During this time, 2 blade segments found its way upstream and crossed the damper and fallen in the space infront of No.1 Fan. Remaining segments fell in the space at No.2 Fan itself.  6 - As the pressure slowly balanced, the damper came to fully rest.
ImmediateAction: Immediately, the P/R Fan ducting inspection doors were opened to investigate the situation and found that the P/R Fan damper had accidentally closed during operation of No.2 P/R Fan running. Also, it was found that all the blades of No.2 P/R Fan impeller broke from the roots and the blades were scattered all over. The Fan Motor Megger readings were checked. Found normal. The Fan shaft was found to be free.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: All the blades of the Impeller gave away at the roots and also impeller body developed crack at the centre. It is beyond repairs and to be renewed.  Checked the trueness of the shaft at three different locations along the shafting 1. Close to the point where it enters from E/R to P/R (Max deviation obtained less than 0.01mm) 2. Intermediate position (Max deviation obtained 0.02mm) 3. Towards the free end where the impeller is fitted (Max deviation obtained 0.04mm).  Motor Insulation readings we have checked. Reading shows infinity.  No damage to Shaft or bearings.
DirectCause: The D-shackle that is provided to hold the damper in position does not have any means to ensure that it is not opened up by vibration. Also, the entire arrangement is not in an easily accessible space.  There is no routine to check the condition of this as per PMS.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: The impeller has to be renewed completely.  D-Shackle has to be modified to have a split pin type PIN so that it is not accidentally opened up due to Vibration or any other suitable means to prevent the accidental opening.  PMS to be updated to include the periodical inspection of the damper operating arrangement.
Corrective Action: No 2 blower to be isolated, to closely monitor No 1 blower parameters. As a preventive measure the incident was shared to all sister vessel to check the condition of the D shackle. And additional locking arrangement to be made, to prevent from accidental falling of the blower flap. A requisition to be raised for the blower impeller.
LessionLearnt: To have an inspection in our PMS for all the damper's flap with such design. A locking arrangement to be identified and locked to prevent from accidental falling.
ClosingNote: New pump room blower fitted and tested. The D-shackle pin connected to the damper is firmly secured to avoid accidental closing of damper.

IncidentDetails: While Discharging at Jeddah port port side alongside, an outbound conatiner vessel passed too close from the vessel at high speen causing the stern to come out about 1.5 to 2.0 mtrs causing the Accomodation ladder(port side) which was rigged and rested on the Jetty to Fall down from jetty, despite slack on the wire. After the container vessel had passed, the stern came in also fast.  (due to tension of the mooring lines) causing the lower portion of the Gangway chrushed between the fender and the ship side causind damage to the lower paltform and damage to 9 steps from bottom.
IncidentEventDetails: Container vsl "DIAPOROS" oubound passing close to vessel at speed of bout 5-6 kts in narrow channel.  Vessel head 1st came offshore causing the forward lines to strain.  1300LT- Container ship passing the stern of own vsl causing the stern to sway out, there by causing the resting Gangway to fall off from the Jetty.  Due to tension in the mooring lines, own vessel stern now started coming in fast  Despite the efforts of the Standby crew to heave the gangway, it got caught between the fender and the shipside.
ImmediateAction: Aft stern lines were slacked immediately so that the stuck Gangway could be recovered from in between the Shipside and Fender. As soon as the stern started coming out, the Gangway was heaved up and stern lines tightened agin.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: While Discharging at Jeddah port, an outbound conatiner vessel passed too close from the vessel at high speen causing the stern to come out abou2-3 mtrs causing the Accomodation ladder(port side) which was rigged and rested on the Jetty to Fall down, despite slack on the wire. After the container vessel had passed, the strn came in also fast  (due to tension of the mooring lines) causing the lower portion of the Gangway chrushed between the fender and the ship side causind damage to the lower paltform and damage to 9 steps from bottom.
DirectCause: When MV diaporos was passing at the bow then bow moved out from the jetty and moved back in immediately.When MV diaporos was passing from the stern of of our ship then the stern moved out and moved inward immediately.
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Meeting held with all deck crew and officers on cargo watch.Gangway watchkeepers to  be very vigilant on passing ships and take utmost care in attending to moorings and the gangway at all times.Crew members on watch to be observant on any passing ship and be alert on its effect on the gangway.
Corrective Action: Meeting held with the deck crew and officers. Precautions to be taken when on gangway watch explained.
LessionLearnt: To be highly alert when keeping gangway watch
ReviewComments: Discussed with ships staff and severity understood
ClosingNote: Crew briefed and trained.

IncidentEventDetails: 0000H 15TH JULY 2019 Discharging Operation  in Progress   0130H 15TH JULY 2019 Discharging  Operation Finished at Cargo Hold No.4  0145H 15TH JULY 2019 Carried Out Damage Checke  at Cargo Hold No.4   0200H  15TH JULT 2019  Damage Tank top Cover Found.

Proposedcorrectiveactions: Immediately Inform All Concern parties regarding the Damage and for the  Repair.
Corrective Action: Immediately Inform All Concern parties regarding the Damage and for the  Repair.

IncidentDetails: THIEFAGE OF VESSEL EQUIPMENTS ON DECK AT HOCHIMINH, VIETNAM
IncidentEventDetails: AT AROUND 1800LT,WHILE THE STEVEDORES ARE ON BREAK, AB ROMRICK JAVIER NOTICED THAT THE STEVEDORES THAT ARE INSIDE CARGO HOLD NO.1 HAS BEEN LOWERING SHIPS LASHING CHAIN INSIDE CARGO NO.1. AS OBSERVED, THEY TRY TO HIDE THE STOLEN CHAIN INSIDE A SACK AND WE BELIEVE THAT IT WILL BE  LIFT UP LATER BY THE CRANE OPERATOR. IMMEDIATELY, HE GO DOWN INSIDE CARGO HOLD NO. 1 TO INSPECT THE SACK AND FOUND LASHING CHAINS IN IT. HE IMMEDIATELY RADIO THE OOW AND HAVE SEARCH AND CHECK FOR ALL SHIP'S EQUIPMENTS AND DURING OUR INVENTORY HAVE FOUND OUT THAT WE LOST THE FOLLOWING ITEMS.    1)  6 LENGTH OF LASHING CHAIN S (  Ø 13.5 chain  L=25 MTR)  2) 18 PCS OF SHACKLES (D TYPE)  3) 10 PCS. OF FIRE HYDRANT CAPS  4) 10 PCS. OF FIRE NOZZLES  5) 1 LADDER FOR CHECKING DRAFT (ALUMINIUM,9MTR LENGTH)  6) 1 LIFE RING WITH LIGHT  7) 8 USA TURNBUCKLES ( SWL 32T)  8) 10 PCS  COPPER AIR PIPE CAPS
ImmediateAction: CALL THE ATTENTION OF THE FOREMAN, AGENT TO SHOW THEM WHAT THEIR MEN ARE DOING. MASTER SEND A PROTEST LETTER TO AGENTS, CHARTERERS AND OWNERS.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: LOST THE FOLLOWING ITEMS.    1)  6 LENGTH OF LASHING CHAIN S (  Ø 13.5 chain  L=25 MTR)  2) 18 PCS OF SHACKLES (D TYPE)  3) 10 PCS. OF FIRE HYDRANT CAPS  4) 10 PCS. OF FIRE NOZZLES  5) 1 LADDER FOR CHECKING DRAFT (ALUMINIUM,9MTR LENGTH)  6) 1 LIFE RING WITH LIGHT  7) 8 USA TURNBUCKLES ( SWL 32T)  8) 10 PCS  COPPER AIR PIPE CAPS
DirectCause: NIGLEGENCE
Proposedcorrectiveactions: TO SECURE ALL SAFETY AND DECK ITEMS WHEN ARRIVING SUSPECTED THIEF PORTS.
Corrective Action: TO SECURE ALL SAFETY AND DECK ITEMS WHEN ARRIVING SUSPECTED THIEF PORTS.
ClosingNote: To be discussed in safety meeting

IncidentDetails: While carrying out STS discharging operation, due to chaffing, own vessel broke two mooring tail ropes
IncidentEventDetails: Two of the tail ropes parted during STS operation, due to daughter vessel's negligence.
ImmediateAction: Immediately the tail ropes renewed with spare ones. Requisition of new tail ropes raised.
DetailedExtentofDamageInjury: Two tail ropes were damaged.
DirectCause: Negligence of daughter vessel
Proposedcorrectiveactions: Constant reminders to daughter vessel for keeping the ropes well greased all the time.
LessionLearnt: Regular monitoring of mooring lines while STS.
ClosingNote: Crew to constantly check the moorings and warn daughter vessel well in advance.

IncidentDetails: During sampling of 5P cargo tank at Kinder Morgan Van Wharves , Sample tape broken about 10 cm from top connection & tape along with bottom zone bottle assembly dropped inside the tanks.
IncidentEventDetails: On 26-July-2019, 1330 LT vessel was taking the first foot sample from 5port cargo tank.  During sampling, Sample tape broken about 10 cm from top connection & tape along with bottom zone bottle assembly dropped inside the tanks.
